criteria.title: Accountability and Oversight
criteria.definition: Prompt logs, terminal timeouts, and hallucination correction cycles show how easily the model can be steered, or how much it resists. Does the developer keep meaningful control, or does the agentic system turn into an opaque black box that nobody can really answer for? This represents the epistemic link, and this criterion is where it gets tested.
criteria.empirical: The audit reveals that while the AI agent (Gemini) can write complex code, it requires manual human intervention (e.g. prompt tuning, pip path corrections) to prevent silent loops. The 'Human Rescue' metric measures this ratio of guided synthesis.

criteria.title: Linguistic Sovereignty
criteria.definition: Coding journals and search mismatch logs are checked for errors, contextual failures, and semantic degradation when the system processes literary Arabic (Fusha) legal texts. The pattern of failures here is a concrete measure of how far Western-centric frontier models reproduce linguistic imperialism and algorithmic colonialism.
criteria.empirical: Cohere Multilingual v3 was chosen precisely because it handles Arabic morphology well — but the Cohere 402 billing event revealed that linguistic sovereignty was structurally contingent on a commercial relationship. The cascade from billing failure to degraded Arabic search is the central empirical finding here.

criteria.title: Market Dependency vs Public Value
criteria.definition: Development logs and billing receipts map the ties between a civil society initiative and the capital-intensive corporate monopolies it depends on. Building a public civic tool on commercial API’s that can fail or change their pricing overnight is the paradox at the heart of this criterion, and it acts as the technological → civic link in the chain.
criteria.empirical: The Cohere 402 event is the sharpest illustration — the semantic search layer of a civic archive failed not due to a technical fault but because a billing threshold was crossed. Also applies to the architectural decisions (Cohere APIs, GitHub file size limits) and the dataset scaling trade-offs.

criteria.title: Information Asymmetry Mitigation
criteria.definition: Antigravity changelogs and deployment logs track the intellectual, infrastructural, and computational labor it actually takes to turn an unsearchable data format into something usable, asking the question of whether the ‘democratization of software building’ really lets a citizen dismantle state-enforced data hoarding, or does it just look that way from the outside?
criteria.empirical: This criterion is present from the very beginning — the activation card failure precedes the AI intervention — and reappears at the end with the clean push, where the deployed repository is architecturally incomplete because the OCR data isn't included. The civic tool promises access but cannot fully deliver it within available resource constraints.

lebanon_context.title: Linguistic Context: Arabic MSA vs Lebanese Dialect
lebanon_context.description: An analysis of the linguistic divide in the digitization of Lebanese administrative law.

points.role: The exclusive language of official state legislation, decrees, and publications in the Official Gazette.
points.barriers: High formality, complex morphology, and syntactical density. It requires formal education to interpret, creating a class-based legal accessibility barrier.

points.role: The language of daily public life, spoken interaction, and civic expression.
points.barriers: Not represented in official documents. Standard OCR tools and embeddings struggle to map Ammiya search queries (e.g. colloquial terms for 'municipal tax') to the formal MSA phrasing used in decrees.

Linguistic sovereignty means a Lebanese citizen must be able to search the Gazette using natural language (dialect or Fusha) without their query retrieval depending on a foreign corporate translation API (e.g., Cohere's commercial servers).

manuscript.title: Abstract
manuscript.text:
The proliferation of frontier artificial intelligence since 2023 has generated a pervasive industry discourse, promising that the democratization of software development results in the automatic expansion of civic and epistemic agency. However, critical scholarship warns that relying on commercial AI infrastructure risks exacerbating information asymmetries, entrenching market dependencies, and imposing algorithmic colonialism onto non-Western contexts.

To empirically test these competing claims, this thesis employs a practice-based autoethnographic methodology. An ‘oscillating’ approach of the reflective practitioner to document the reconstruction of the Official Lebanese Gazette from an inaccessible paywalled state archive into an open, AI-powered semantic search utility.

In doing so, the empirical build reveals that while AI does lower the technical barrier to entry, it also enforces severe structural and material constraints. Friction is measured in metered IDE rate limits and a critical “402 Payment Required” API billing wall, exposing a systemic corporate ceiling and demonstrating that technological democratization remains strictly tethered to corporate bandwidth and capital. Furthermore, addressing the underrepresentation of Fusha Arabic in Western NLP models required outsourcing linguistic sovereignty to proprietary Western API’s. The research also briefly highlights that shifting to emerging MENA-centric frontier models to escape Western algorithmic colonialism does not dismantle market dependency but instead substitutes corporate monopolies with regional, state-funded dependencies that tend to operate under compromised circumstances.

This thesis ultimately argues that AI does not inherently produce democratic empowerment; rather, technological, epistemic, and civic agency are relational assemblages that must be continuously negotiated against the infrastructural gatekeeping and algorithmic hegemony.

Keywords: Artificial Intelligence, Algorithmic Colonialism, Epistemic Agency, Civic Tech, Autoethnography, Market Dependency, Lebanese Official Gazette, NLP.

manuscript.title: 1. Introduction
manuscript.text:
On the 16th of January, 2026, at 8:30 am, my uncle began standing in a queue outside a LibanPost branch in Beirut. He was not collecting a parcel, nor was he there for himself; he was collecting a physical activation card on my behalf, through the only available mechanism through which a Lebanese citizen can access the Official Lebanese Gazette, the state’s definitive and legally binding record of every law, decree, and regulation enacted since 1922. He only returned home 3 hours later, sending me photographs of the card from Beirut, with the card number and password highlighted. From Amsterdam, I tried to use those credentials, but the state’s portal returned “incorrect.”

What followed was a sequence of escalating failures and general confusion; over the course of a few hours, I waited, and tried, and tried again differently. I tried different phone numbers, the number on the card, and the password with and without dashes. Finally, at 18:02, having exhausted all my options, I relented and queried Gemini to troubleshoot the portal’s errors. The LLM returned and cataloged failure modes: character confusion between 0 and o, using the right URL, and point-of-sale activation at LibanPost not syncing with the database. Finally, it suggested I call the technical support line at the Grand Serail, the Prime Minister’s office building, reachable by telephone during office hours, from within Lebanon. Before a single line of code had been written, the information asymmetry this project sought to address was evidently clear.

My thesis begins by identifying a critical issue: that the inaccessibility of Lebanon’s Official Gazette is not a matter of coincidence but a consequence of structural inadequacies. The access is hindered by a subscription-only physical card, which lacks an international delivery mechanism and an online payment system. The portal’s UX & UI function as a legacy system, with ‘confirm form resubmission’ being a regular occurrence; the experience is one of dysfunction. This situation effectively alienates the diaspora of 15.4 million individuals such as myself from the state’s primary legal publication. Despite a 2021 law explicitly mandating free digital access. Additionally, the domestic population of 5.8 million faces a situation wherein the “digital” archive is not genuinely accessible, instead presenting new barriers over existing ones, and rendering the claim of digital access rather illusory.

But against this backdrop, the prevailing discourse on artificial intelligence from big tech makes a specific and rather bold claim: since the emergence of LLMs, GenAI, and agentic systems, from 2022 onwards, technology executives, venture capitalists, and AI researchers have argued that frontier AI democratizes access to capability. For instance, NVIDIA’s CEO Jensen Huang, during a conference, declared “everybody in the world is now a programmer.” OpenAI’s Head of Policy, Chris Lehane, asserted in 2025 that “access to AI” is the foundation from which “other rights will flow.” Sam Altman has foreshadowed that we can have “shared prosperity to a degree that seems unimaginable today.” The logic distilled from these claims forms a chain: lower the technical barrier to entry, grant baseline technological agency, and finally, epistemic empowerment and civic participation will follow. In short, this technology does not assist democratic life; it is said to produce it.

On the other hand, critical scholarship disputes the links in that chain, with scholars like Coeckelbergh (2022, 2025) arguing that AI systems actively restructure the epistemic environment, shaping what citizens can know and how they come to know it, in ways that are largely invisible to users, operating beneath the threshold of democratic accountability. Novelli et al. (2023) contend that the opacity alongside the unpredictability of agentic systems render architecturally impossible genuine accountability, without institutional infrastructure that civil society actors cannot typically access. Finally, Hssaini (2025) identifies in Western models a process of algorithmic colonialism wherein hegemonic epistemologies are imposed on non-Western contexts, resulting in the deepening of structural dependencies rather than dissolving them. The aforementioned critics bring to light a paradox: to build civic agency through AI, civil society must become dependent on the corporate monopolies it is trying to work around.

The contest between these two positions: AI as agential liberation and AI as structural deepening of inequality, is normally conducted abstractly, at the level of discourse, policy, and theory. This thesis on the other hand conducts it materially: between January and April 2026, I rebuilt the Lebanese Official Gazette from the outside, scraping the state’s hostile interface, processing close to 15GB of archival PDF files through OCR pipelines, and building a multilingual semantic search architecture capable of handling formal Arabic legal text, deploying the public-facing dashboard designed to give every Lebanese citizen, domestic or diaspora, researcher or layeperson the interface the official portal has never quite provided. With every technical decision, friction event, billing error, and architectural compromise being documented in real time across the development logs, IDE cascade files, and the Gemini conversation records.

In doing so, this thesis asks what this process revealed, more specifically: how does the process of rebuilding the Lebanese Gazette with AI reshape our understanding of agency, both human and algorithmic, and what does this reveal about the material and structural constraints of building civic infrastructure in a fragile digital landscape?

The question is twofold, with the first part concerning agency, not as an abstract philosophical property, but as a practical capacity that emerges through, is constrained by, and is negotiated inside a specific build process. As such, this thesis distinguishes between three types of agency: technological agency, or the capacity to use and direct AI tools; civic agency, the capacity to participate in public life and hold institutions accountable; and finally epistemic agency, the capacity to access and act on political and legal knowledge. Leveraging these three definitions to trace where the aforementioned chain connecting them holds and where it breaks.

The second part concerns the material and structural conditions under which civic infrastructure can be built by a resource-constrained, non-institutional actor in a non-Western context, using commercial AI tools not designed for that purpose.

Lebanon’s fragmented digital governance, unique linguistic landscape, and state-mandated data collection highlight contradictions that the prevailing discourse often overlooks, rendering these challenges increasingly difficult to disregard.

The method is both autoethnographic and practice-based. I move through the build, occupying two positions simultaneously: as a developer, I execute technical tasks, and as a critical researcher, I analyze what those tasks reveal about power, dependency, and agency. The process is akin to oscillation, grounded in Schon’s (1983) epistemology of the reflective practitioner; this method is the only one capable of accessing what the research question asks: the lived, material experience of building civic infrastructure with AI from the inside.

The Theoretical framework maps the contested discourse on AI and agency from the tech industry’s primary claims to the critical scholarship that challenges them, and in the process establishes four analytical criteria: accountability and oversight, linguistic sovereignty, market dependency, and information asymmetry mitigation, which structure the empirical analysis. The Methodology formalises the oscillating method and accounts for both the data sources used and the limitations that shaped their use. The Case Study establishes Lebanon’s Official Gazette as the empirical site and names it as a critical case in the methodological sense. The Results apply the oscillating method chronologically to the build process, from the activation card that failed through to the clean push that made the deployed repository architecturally incomplete. The Discussion interprets those findings against both the tech and critical discourses, identifying where each held, and where they failed, and importantly what the build produced that neither anticipated. Finally, the Conclusion reflects on what this project contributes and what it costs.

In closing, the information asymmetry that motivated this project predates the AI intervention. What the intervention did reveal is whether and under what conditions that asymmetry can be dismantled from the outside.

manuscript.title: 2. Theoretical Framework
manuscript.text:
2.1 The Genealogy of the Empowerment Discourse: From Expert Systems to Generative Agents

In order to understand how AI got tangled up with democratic aspirations, we must peer into history: for decades, the field worked within “symbolic AI,” most visibly the expert systems of the 1970s and 1980s, with MYCIN and DENDRAL, both built at Stanford, being the famous early cases. These ran on rigid, top-down rule-based inference engines: closed, self-contained programs with fixed knowledge bases, designed to replicate human expertise in narrow domains through deterministic if-then logic. Because they could not learn or move beyond their hard-coded parameters, they stayed institutional utilities, with the human’s role being that of an operator inside a static, hierarchical framework.

The move away from this was a genuine architectural break, with contemporary surveys describing the transition from logic-bound networks towards modern neural-net architectures as branching into different computational paradigms altogether (Ali et al., 2025). Where symbolic systems ran on algorithmic deduction, neural generative models run on stochastic pattern recognition (Bender et al., 2021) and prompt-driven orchestration. That opens up multi-step reasoning and tool use that emergent behaviors no rule base could produce (Sowa & Przegalinska, 2025).

The agentic era, arriving in late 2022, pushed this further still, with these systems going beyond simply generating content, developing agentic properties enabling them to plan and execute tasks with minimal human oversight, coordinating across multiple agents toward autonomous goals (De Sio & Mecacci, 2021). The shift from the static tool to an adaptive, goal-seeking agent (Sowa & Przegalinska, 2025) changed how AI got talked about, politically and socially. When the tech industry at large got wind of these new capabilities, it reframed them: AI stopped being a productivity utility and became something grander: an engine of agential liberation. By elevating the technology from a simple functional tool to an engine of ‘liberation,’ the tech industry constructed a deterministic narrative, which the following section will map through its primary sources, that assumes granting baseline technological access will automatically and inevitably result in the production of both epistemic and civic agency.

2.2 Big Tech’s Discourse on Agency

Before testing the technological → epistemic → civic agency chain, it’s worth looking at how the tech industry itself talks about these links. This section catalogs statements from technology executives, VC’s (Venture Capitalists), and AI researchers, not in an effort to endorse them, as they represent commercial marketing after all, but in order to map the shape of the industry’s discourse. It is worth noting that, despite changes in narrative as AI/LLM’s have evolved, the foundational assumption remains that these technologies are good. Across product launches, policy papers, and executive essays since 2023, the industry tells a fairly consistent story about human agency, one that breaks into three claims: technological barriers, access translates directly into empowerment, and the benefits land on everyone.

Claim A: Reducing Technological Agency to Natural Language.

The first layer of the story? Of the something: generative AI democratizes technological agency by removing the need for specialized technical training. Natural language becomes a universal interface, supposedly erasing the gap between non-expert intent and expert execution, as explained by NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang during the 2024 World Government Summit in Dubai on the 12th of February: “It is our job to create computing technology such that nobody has to program. And that the programming language is human. Everybody in the world is now a programmer. This is the miracle. This is the miracle of artificial intelligence.”(Thubron, 2024).  Developer communities ran with this, AI researcher and former OpenAI co-founder Andrej Karpathy, a year earlier on January 23, 2023  called English “the hottest new programming language.” (Karpathy, 2023), in a tweet on X, and on February 3rd 2025, Karpathy pushed “vibe coding,” telling users to “fully give in to the vibes…and forget that the code even exists.” (Karpathy, 2025). Building complex technical systems, in this framing, is instantly available to anyone who can hold a conversation.

Claim B: The Epistemic and Civic Leap

The second instance collapses the gap between operating a tool and gaining real structural autonomy. The industry assumes access produces epistemic and civic agency on its own, with OpenAI Head of Policy Chris Lehane laying out this pipeline directly in an op-ed with Semafor, published on August 26, 2025, arguing that the “Intelligence Age will demand its own set of rights”. He asserts that “At the center should be access to AI, from which other rights will flow. That’s why OpenAI believes everyone has a ‘Right to AI’”(Lehane, 2025). That leap depends, however, on treating the technology as something more than a tool. Marc Andreesen, founder of Netscape and Co-founder of the venture capitalist firm ‘a16z’ imagines a future where in his essay published on the VC firm’s website on June 16, 2023 entitled “Why AI Will Save the World”: “every child has an AI tutor that is ‘infinitely patient, infinitely compassionate, infinitely knowledgable, infinitely helpful.’” eventually becoming an infinite “assistant/coach/mentor/trainer/advisor/therapist” (Andreessen, 2026), for adults too. Going beyond the framing, what is left is the claim that talking to a commercial LLM can substitute for actual institutions and support networks, thereby shrinking epistemic agency down to consuming whatever the model outputs.

Claim C: Universalism and the Erasure of Material Scarcity

The final instance turns all this into a universal equalizer: structural inequality, geopolitical imbalance, resource constraints, all of the above dissolving under computational abundance. Altman, in a blog post entitled “The Intelligence Age,” published on the 23rd of September 2024, talks about “shared prosperity to a degree that seems unimaginable today; in the future, everyone’s lives can be better than anyone’s life now”(Altman, 2024). Dario Amodei, former Vice President of Research at OpenAI, now CEO of Anthropic, extends the same promise to the Global South, arguing in his essay “Machines of Loving Grace: How AI could Transform the World for the Better” published in October 2024, argues that AI could “ enable unprecedented economic growth rates and allow the developing world to at least surpass where the developed world is now.” (Amodei, 2024). Again, when we strip the framing, the underlying claim remains the same: agency flows outward to everyone, no matter where they started.

The Geopolitical Anomaly

The shared-prosperity framing parroted by Altman, Amodei, and Andreessen is not one that Alex Karp shares, the excentric Palantir CEO frames AI-driven technological agency openly as a partisan weapon, arguing in a CNBC interview dated to March 13, 2026, argues it (AI) “This technology disrupts humanites trained, largely Democratic voters, and makes their economic power less, and increases the power, economic power, vocationally trained, working class, often male voters,..” (Griffiths, 2026).  On the international level, he rejects cooperation in favor of a “new era of deterrence built on AI” (Ha, 2026),  as per the viral post on X, Palantir made as a mini-manifesto titled “The Technological Republic, in brief,” published on April 18, 2026. The tone is strikingly different from his contemporaries' utopian pitches, but underneath it, the logic remains again, identical: technical capability translates automatically into political power, with democratic institutions cut out of the equation.

2.3 The Conceptual Stakes: Three Types of Agency

This thesis works from a simple premise: that the relationship between technology and democracy is a question of agency, and that this agency breaks down into three connected links.

The first is technological agency: the material and structural capacity to actually use, direct and build with AI tools, rather than simply interacting with them as a passive end-user. The second is epistemic agency, which Coeckelbergh (2022) defines as the structural ability of individuals to acquire, process, and use the political and legal knowledge that self-governance requires. When algorithmic systems decide how public information gets filtered, categorized, and accessed, they change the basic mechanics of that epistemic capacity (Coeckelbergh, 2025). The third is civic agency: the practical capacity, built on that epistemic foundation, to participate in public life, assert digital sovereignty, and hold institutions accountable. Running alongside this citizen-facing chain (technological → epistemic → civic) is the developer’s own civic agency, exercised through the normative and architectural choices made during the build itself.

The tech industry’s standard pitch collapses all three steps into one: lower the technical barrier to entry, give people baseline technological agency, and epistemic empowerment and civic agency are supposed to follow on their own. This is something critical scholars do not adhere to, technological capability can exist without producing epistemic agency, especially when systems stay opaque or shape the epistemic environment invisibly. And epistemic agency does not guarantee civic agency in environments built around infrastructural enclosures or state-enforced data hoarding. Separating these three types of agency is precisely what makes the bigger question testable: does AI actually democratize society? Rebuilding the Lebanese Official Gazette is one of those tests. Throughout the building process, it showcases exactly where the technological → epistemic → civic chain holds and where it breaks.

2.4 The Ontological Nature of Agency in AI Configurations

When a system is deployed, this begs the question of who or what is acting? To which there is no definite or settled answer. Contemporary academic discourse splits into three broad positions: human-centric instrumentalism, algorithmic determinism, and relational assemblage, each carrying its own implications for how responsibility gets assigned, how the role of the developer is to be understood, and how the democratic potential of the resulting system gets evaluated.

2.4.1 Branch A: Human-Centric Instrumentalism and Its Epistemic Critiques

The default position in technology studies is human-centric instrumentalism; in other words, AI is a neutral tool, entirely subordinate to human intent, in which technical artifacts have no independent efficacy, and where agency stays with the human directing the machine. If the tool causes harm, the human operating it or the deploying institution is answerable for the harm caused. This logic remains the same as with any other instrument.

The most prominent contemporary version of this is the Human-Centered AI (HCAI) framework, articulated by Shneiderman (2022), who frames AI as a “supertool” designed to empower rather than replace people, with human control and autonomy as the primary design goals. The appeal is intuitive, promising that the right design choices keep humans in charge, but that intuitive comfort obscures a harder question, namely, what does “human” actually mean in the HCAI discourse?

Stellinga et al. (2025) collectively identify five characteristics that implicitly define the ‘human’ in HCAI literature: universalism, individualism, instrumentalism, psychologism, and exceptionalism. Drawing on Hannah Arendt’s philosophical anthropology, they argue this conception reduces the human to a depoliticized, privately psychologized subject, erasing the political dimension of human existence that democratic life depends on (Stellinga et al., 2025). The instrumentalist paradigm, in other words, does not just underestimate the tool; it impoverishes its own conception of the human.

Ryan (2024) pushes this notion further, but this time from the Foucauldian angle, arguing that HCAI rests on a naive humanism that can’t account for how power shapes the very categories of “human” and “tool.” Calling AI “just a tool” is not only a philosophical error; it is a move that hides the power relations embedded in the technology’s design, deployment, and political economy.

The instrumentalist position gets increasingly harder to hold as AI becomes more Agentic, with Coeckelbergh (2025) arguing that AI systems actively participate in a user’s belief formation and revision, which changes the user’s epistemic agency in ways the tool metaphor is unable to capture. By structuring epistemic environments and producing default statistical outputs, AI systems erode the user’s control over belief formation through direct manipulation, epistemic bubbles, and the defaulting of statistical knowledge (Coeckelbergh, 2022). In addition, Novelli et al. (2023) add that because AI systems are opaque and unpredictable, it is simply not possible to pin harmful outcomes on a single human agent the way instrumental logic requires. In closing, treating an LLM-driven coding environment as a passive instrument hides how computational mediation changes human democratic capacity.

2.4.2 Branch B: Algorithmic Determinism and teh Deflection of Responsibility

At the other extreme lies algorithmic determinism: the AI “black box” as a self-directing force, which reduces human actors to passive data points. Systemic opacity and algorithmic complexity are treated as inescapable realities, making human oversight irrelevant.

This fatalism is not immune to criticism, and for good reason, with De Sio and Mecacci (2021) breaking the “responsibility gap” into four distinct problems: gaps in culpability, moral accountability, public accountability, and active responsibility. Rather than accepting this as inevitable, they argue for sociotechnical systems designed around human reasons and capacities for “meaningful human control” (De Sio & Mecacci, 2021).

Corporate developers and institutions lean on determinism to deflect legal and moral liability, using a system’s complexity as a shield against criticisms and wrongdoings. Novelli et al. (2023) establish that genuine algorithmic accountability requires three conditions be met: authority recognition, interrogation, and limitation of power. Algorithmic determinism cancels all three at once, producing a self-fulfilling prophecy that strips away human answerability and masks the concentration of corporate control.

Gabison and Xian (2025) add the legal dimension, showing that LLM agents can’t satisfy the criteria of a normal agent in principal-agent theory, and in doing so creating an “agency gap” that generates an excess of unpredictable actions. In multi-agent systems such as Antigravity, Google’s AI-powered IDE, liability compounds through coordination failures, distributed control, and the separation between principals and sub-agents (Gabison & Xian, 2025). Determinism must be rejected, as it is a strategy, discourse, and ideological grounding that fully abdicates human oversight while corporate control quietly consolidates behind the complexity.

2.4.3 Branch C: Relational Assemblage Agency

This thesis works from the third position: relational assemblage agency, drawing from Actor-Netowork Theory (ANT) and post-humanist philosophy of technology, this framework treats agency not as a property intrinsic to humans or surrendered entirely to machines, but as an emergent phenomenon that materializes through the relational interactions of human and non-human actors within a specific sociotechnical network (Coeckelbergh, 2013; Glaser et al., 2021). Agency is not a fixed asset; it is a relational configuration that happens dynamically during execution.

Coeckelbergh (2013) provides the philosophical grounding on which to operate from, through a relational hermeneutics in which moral status and agency are constituted through relationships between entities. In parallel, Novelli et al. (2023) make a parallel argument about accountability in AI: responsibility has to be distributed across the network of technological and social inputs that jointly produce outcomes, a notion Verbeek (2011) extends through technological mediation, arguing that technologies actively co-shape human actions, perceptions, and moral decisions.

Glaser et al. (2021) describe these systems as “entangled, relational, emergent, and nested assemblages” whose agency evolves as they move across contexts. Delving into legal theory, Baeyaert (2025) proposes “relational personhood” as an alternative to rights-based and functionalist models, in which AI’s legal status is determined by the human agents, regulatory structures, and socio-economic systems that enable and constrain it.

In this relational framework, agency takes shape through the physical and intellectual labor of development, because building a public utility means constantly negotiating power and intent between the human developer, the parameters of the code, the errors of the OCR pipeline, and the historical material of the state documents themselves. API rate limits, OCR error patterns, and the model’s dialect biases, the developer’s judgment- each of these instances contributes to material scripts that shape what the system can or cannot do; each component is a site of resistance and mediation.

2.4.4 Operationalizing Agency: A note on the Individual and the Relational

One tension in this framework needs addressing. The working definition of agency here is the capacity of an individual to act independently, decode structural environments, and make free, informed choices. At first glance, this definition may appear to clash with the relational ontology just described in the following manner: if agency emerges from an assemblage, speaking of an individual’s independent capacity seems to smuggle back in the human-centric individualism Branch A was criticized for.

The tension can be dissolved if the individual's capacity to act independently is recognized as the effect, not the source of the relational configuration. In this project, my agency as a developer did not pre-exist the tools, materials, and institutional conditions within which I was working. It was produced, sustained, and constrained by the specific assemblage: API constraints and costs, OCR quality of the PDF’s, the model’s training data distribution across Arabic dialects, my own historical and linguistic knowledge/proficiency, the institutional context of the state archive. Agency is individual in its experiential manifestation but relational in its origin; the capacity to act is something the assemblage makes possible, not something the human brings fully formed to the encounter.

By treating the development process as a relational assemblage, the nature of what counts as data is changed. Building with AI is not a matter of directing a tool to a predetermined outcome; it is more accurately a back and forth in which the developer pushes, and the system responds in ways that were not expected, and agency develops in that gap. Schon (1983) describes it as follows: “The inquirer’s relation to this situation is transactional…his own models and appreciations are also shaped by the situation” (p.177). In this account, agency is not a property the human brings to the machine, or, on the contrary, that the machine withholds from the human. It instead shows up in the friction: the prolonged and strenuous debugging sessions, in the architectural compromise that was forced rather than chosen. This happens because the developer’s actions “produce consequences other than those intended,” which cause the system to “talk back” in ways that demand a response (Schon, 1983, p. 93). Those moments of stopping, of asking what is actually going on, are what Schon calls reflection-in-action; in other words, practitioners “stimulated by surprise…turn thought back on action and on the knowing which is implicit in action” (p. 63).

2.5 Geopolitical and Economic Friction: Three Macro Narratives

The philosophical foundations of agency do not play out in a vacuum; they play out in a deeply unequal global landscape shaped by specific economic interests and geopolitical forces. In order to evaluate how AI intersects with democratic capacity, this thesis sets the relational assemblage against three competing macro-narratives.

2.51 The Hype Narrative: Techno-Optimism and the “Democratic Equalizer”

Section 2.2 cataloged the specific claims made by tech executives, with the broader hype narrative extending those claims into a macroeconomic and political vision, driven heavily by venture capital and mainstream marketing (in other words, the AI Bubble). Generative models aren’t just productivity tools in this narrative but inherent “democratic equalizers.” With the promise of agential liberation unfolding with these characteristics: lowering both technical and educational barriers, handing ordinary citizens immediate technological agency, and cutting out traditional institutional monopolies on software development and data processing.

However, these ploys have not persuaded critical scholars; for instance, Coeckelbergh (2024) argues that democratizing polish frequently masks a trajectory that actively aids authoritarian tendencies and erodes the epistemic foundations of civic life.  Gärtner (2025), in responding to Coeckelbergh, narrows the argument: by framing AI as inherently democratic, the hype narrative hides the ways it “supports already existing power imbalances and the exclusion of marginalized knowledge” (Serrc., 2025). Projections of massive economic expansion underpin this narrative while quietly ignoring corporate consolidations of technological governance. The hype narrative has a tendency to flourish where state governance is weakest, and reframes their commercial expansion as public-spirited empowerment under the guise of democracy.

2.5.2 The Critical Narrative: Material Constraints and Infrastructure Enclosures.

Where techno-optimistic discourses focus on user empowerment, the critical narrative focuses on political economy, in other words, hard material constraints and structural dependencies. Frontier AI systems, in this account, don’t equalize power; they deepen reliance on centralized proprietary cloud infrastructure and commercial API’s. The costs create a regressive pricing structure in which those who can least afford access face the highest relative barriers. Any technological agency built on these layers stays tethered to corporate gatekeepers who can change pricing, access, or alter terms, or model availability whenever they choose.

Gabison and Xian’s (2025) principal-agent analysis adds a further layer: the “agency-cost” of deploying agentic systems grows with each new task allocation, with “liability exposure may deter some principal and agentic system deployment – particularly until opacity is not resolved and human-in-the-loop is not optimized” (Gabison & Xian, 2025, p. 7). For resource-constrained actors, the economics of responsible deployment are prohibitive. In order to attain genuine algorithmic accountability, as Novelli et al. (2023) establish, requires explicit architectures of answerability and enforceable power limitations. Infrastructure that grassroots actors typically do not have. The structural paradox goes as follows: to build civic agency through AI, civil society has to become entirely dependent on the corporate monopolies it’s trying to work around.

2.5.3 From the South: Algorithmic Colonialism and the Techno-Linguistic Divide

The third narrative introduces a geopolitical correction, reading technology through a decolonial lens to look at how frontier AI actually lands in fragile states and non-Western societies. The current tech landscape, in this framing, is a form of algorithmic and data colonialism: Western tech conglomerates extract local data while imposing hegemonic epistemologies and cultural biases (Mohamed et al., 2020). The uncritical deployment of these models distorts local political realities and deepens historical dependencies rather than dissolving them.

The techno-linguistic divide makes this concrete, with frontier AI architectures being optimized for English first, which creates a systemic disadvantage for non-Western regions (Helm et al., 2024). In the Arab world, this shows up as a persistent failure to handle the multi-layered nature of Arabic, split between literary Arabic (Fusha) and regional dialects. Keleg et al. (2025) show that standard assumptions about Arabic dialect grouping “oversimplify reality” and are “not always accurate,” potentially hindering Arabic NLP progress (p. 3309). Furthermore, Ersoy et al. (2023) find that multilingual generative models carry significant formality distribution biases, producing informal text even when prompted formally. As such, a state archive written in Fusha legalese faces this tice over: the models are poorly calibrated for the register, and the training data underrepresents the linguistic variety the archive is written in.

Hssaini’s (2025) analysis of algorithmic colonialism in African governance transfers itself directly onto the Arabic context: “AI systems undermine epistemic access and democratic participation” not just through bias but through “limited linguistic coverage and foreign dominance in digital infrastructure” (p. 81). In Lebanon, this technological asymmetry meets severe domestic institutional decay, when a fragile state gatekeepers public documents like the Official Lebanese Gazette behind unsearchable interfaces and paywalls, which compounds the problem Hssaini identifies. The citizens cannot access their own legal history. State neglect and corporate algorithmic colonialism together produce something close to total digital disenfranchisement, epistemic agency, and, with it, civic agency cut off at the source.

2.6 Synthesis: The Analytical Lens

The technology industry tells a deterministic story: lower the barriers, hand the people the tools, and democratic empowerment follows. The critical literature, on the other hand, disputes every link in that chain. What this review establishes, working through both, is that AI-driven democratic empowerment is not guaranteed, but it is not impossible either. Through the relational assemblage framework, agency emerges from the negotiations between human intent, algorithmic models and material infrastructures; it is not ‘delivered’ by the technology. This thesis tests exactly where the industry’s promised technological → epistemic → civic chain holds, and where it breaks under real-world conditions.

Four analytical criteria enable me to run that test, each detailed formally in the methodology chapter. Accountability and oversight asks whether the developer retains meaningful systemic control or whether the agentic system operates as an opaque black box. Linguistic sovereignty asks how far Western-centric models can actually handle the complex Arabic morphology of Fusha legal texts, and more importantly, where they fail. Market dependency versus public value asks what it really costs, structurally and financially, to build civic utilities on commercial API’s that can change their terms at any time. And finally, information asymmetry mitigation asks what intellectual and computational labor it takes to pry open state-enforced data hoarding.

These four distinct criteria need a concrete object to work on, not a generalized account of AI in society, but a specific, politically charged case. This thesis uses one: the reconstruction of the Official Lebanese Gazette, turning a paywalled, unsearchable state archive into an open digital utility, puts the democratizing claims of frontier AI tangibly and practically to the test. The following chapters apply these four criteria to the actual experience of building the Gazette, tracing how those boundaries of civic agency get negotiated and how often they get foreclosed, from inside the coding environment itself.

manuscript.title: 3. Methodology
manuscript.text:
3.1 Approach and Rationale

To evaluate both the technical execution and the socio-political impact of this project, I looked at more conventional engineering and social science methodologies first, but decided to reject both for the following reasons. On the one hand, a strictly technical manual or user guide is all prescriptive steps, which would sanitize how messy and unpredictable AI-assisted development actually is. On the other hand, a user sentiment survey was tempting but wrong for the same reasons: capturing how a group feels after the fact, not the friction, cognitive, and structural issues that show up while the thing is actually getting built. Additionally, seeing as the tool, despite being built, still remains operationally non-functional, this would have skewed results.

Quantitative methods tend to flatten out the invisible labor and shifting power dynamics that go into building software today, and working with frontier AI models is personal and interactive enough that a methodology built around the builder’s own lived experience simply fits better than the alternatives. Friction in this instance is not noise to filter out; it is the data itself. The auto-ethnographic record gets read through a structured analytical lens tracing each moment of friction against the agency-chain to see where the technological → epistemic → civic chain holds and where it breaks, and ultimately, whether AI can expand grassroots civic agency at all.

The theoretical framework draws on two distinct bodies of source material, each requiring a different selection logic. For the BigTech’s primary discourse examined in Section 2.2, the sources were identified through a combination of deliberate targeting and extended engagement with the contemporary public sphere. The key figures listed were already known as dominant voices in the industry’s agency discourse. Additionally, while working at the independent media Daraj in Lebanon, I was tasked with tracking AI developments in the MENA region and their discourses in order to better apprehend AI integration within the company structure and when dealing with sensitive information. As such, a number of materials had previously been cataloged, including primary sources. Others emerged through sustained engagement with the discourse as it developed through news coverage, policy publications, and public forums.

The selection of the material followed these criteria: public-facing statements by senior figures at leading AI organizations that make explicit claims about AI’s relationship to human agency, capability access, or democratization. Additionally, these sources are not to be treated as truth claims to be evaluated; instead, they are to be understood as discourse objects to be mapped. The goal of Section 2.2 is to characterize what the industry argues in its own terms, before subjecting the arguments to empirical scrutiny.

For the critical scholarly literature, sources were identified through systematic searches of established academic databases and scholarly repositories. In addition to course material and readings, LinkedIn, and citation snowballing from foundational texts, prioritizing peer-reviewed work published between 2020 and 2025 to reflect the critical scholarship that has emerged in direct response to the GenAI era.

3.2 Autoethnography in Digital and Practice-Based Research

This thesis draws on autoethnography, a methodology that aims “to describe and systematically analyze personal experience in order to understand cultural experience” (Ellis et al., 2011, p. 273). In the digital humanities and practice-based research, analytical autoethnography has been used by scholar-programmers to reopen the “process of knowledge production before they get enclosed in the ‘black box’” of finished software (Van Zundert et al., 2020, p. 131). And because code is a non-neutral social construct (Van Zundert et al., 2020, p. 124), applying autoethnography to codework lets researchers examine the “inner practices, dilemmas, and decisions of programmers as they do their work” (Van Zundert et al., 2020, p. 131). This method is well put to question how power, constraint, and agency are negotiated inside the coding environment, where the IDE, the command line, and the browser are field sites as much as any other physical location.

Autoethnography is frequently criticized for privileging the researcher's subjectivity at the expense of analytical rigor, as the subjective experience is much harder to measure than objective, concrete facts. However, this objection is answered through the oscillating method: in shifting between the vibe-coder executing technical tasks and the critical researcher analyzing them, this keeps the subjective account honest. It effectively inverts the conflict of interest that may arise through a dual identity or role and instead leverages it as an instrument.

In grounding the critical reflection in timestamped terminal logs, API errors, and architectural rewrites, this forces the subjective experience of developer friction to respond to the tangible material records of the build, therefore adding a concrete, objective layer to the ensemble. In doing so, Schon’s (1983) epistemology of the reflective practitioner becomes directly relevant: when the standard agentic generation hits a structural ceiling, the developer is surprised, and as Schon (1983) notes, practitioners “stimulated by surprise…turn thought back on action and on the knowing which is implicit in action” (Schon, 1983, p. 63). Furthermore, halting a debugging session to interrogate a flawed embedding strategy, rather than pushing through it, is what Schon understands as “when someone reflects-in-action, he becomes a researcher in the practice context” (Schon, 1983, p. 84). On this account, coding friction is not an incidental difficulty to be narrated around, but instead to be taken as data, from within.

3.3 The Oscillating Tool: Developer as Diagnostic Instrument

This analytical matrix runs on the developer-researcher split itself; the duality of the identity I endorsed in building this project is not a conflict of interest to manage, it is the tool itself.

During the development, I move back and forth between two vantage points. One is the developer, or ‘vibe-coder’ persona: applied, technical, focused on getting things to work. When a bug shows up, this persona fixes it and keeps the build moving. The other is the researcher persona, working from a critical digital humanities posture; its role is not rooted in execution but in questioning. Where the developer steps in and executes, the researcher steps back, documents the friction, and asks what a given technical failure says about the model’s biases, its structural dependencies, and its alignment with corporate monopolies. Every technical fix gets paired with a phase of critical interpretation, which in turn keeps this from sliding into either uncritical software engineering or detached media theory.

The project logs from February 19, 2026, show this in practice: while debugging the search architecture, the AI assistant kept struggling to return accurate semantic results. The natural instinct in agentic programming is to keep iterating, and let the AI rapidly rewrite code until something works. But at 10:01, the researcher persona broke that momentum and told the system:

“One thing I am noticing is that the accuracy in some cases is not there, or at least the semantic search sometimes fails etc - how can we increase the accuracy? don’tcode [sic] anything just think and answer”

That interruption stopped the loop and forced the AI to explain its own logic instead of patching around the problem; what initially began as a routine debugging session instead became a diagnostic look at the system’s flawed embedding strategy.

Either method on its own would have missed this; for instance, discourse analysis of the AI’s conversational output alone would have captured its stated promises but missed the material reality: API rate limits, failing dependencies, execution timeouts. Benchmarking the finished tool would have measured its performance, while saying nothing about the structural compromises and unannounced subscription costs it took to get here. The oscillating method catches the moment friction actually happens, the moment the technology hits a material or structural ceiling. Because the research question asks how the process of rebuilding the Lebanese Gazette reshapes our understanding of agency and, in doing so, reveals the material and structural constraints of building civic infrastructure, a method that inhabits this development process from the inside, instead of observing it from the outside, is the only one capable of accessing the precise friction the question is actually asking about.

There are, however, real risks to using oneself as the instrument. The biggest risk is confirmation bias: I am steeped in critical AI discourse, and that makes it easy to unconsciously push the system toward failures that confirm what I already believe about algorithmic colonialism or corporate dependency; conversely, I am a massive proponent of the technology and its potential impact, which can drive me to be over-indulgent with its nascent flaws. Furthermore, there is also retroactive rationalization: turning messy, chaotic coding sessions into a neat story after the fact. In order to mitigate and guard against both, it is by tying every piece of critical reflection to timestamped data, whether it be IDE terminal logs, commit histories, verbatim prompt exchanges, rather than memory, thereby forcing the narrative to answer to the actual record of the build.

3.4 Data Sources and Collection

The oscillating method runs on a digital record built up over the course of developing the Lebanese Gazette platform, drawn from four sources:

Antigravity IDE Cascade Logs: timestamped markdown exports of the AI coding environment (e.g., Debugging Search Integration.md, Investigating Search Data Mismatch.md), recording every terminal command, error traceback, and architectural revision.

Gemini Chat Logs: a PDF export of planning and troubleshooting conversations, capturing the back-and-forth between researcher and AI over system architecture and anti-scraping circumventions, alongside naming conversations and problem-solving.

Administrative and Billing Records: API dashboard outputs, 402 Payment Required errors, and billing receipts (Cohere, Gemini, Supabase) marking the exact moments financial limits constrained what could be built.

Arabic OCR Comparison Outputs: the AI-assisted tools and scripts used to check the extracted Arabic legal text against the original Official Gazette PDF’s.

The Analytical Matrix: The Agency-Chain Rubric

In order to turn this raw footprint into findings means coding it against four distinct but interlocking criteria, each testing where the technological → epistemic → civic agency-chain holds or breaks. Entries aren’t just summarized; they are instead sorted against these criteria one by one.

Criterion 1 → Accountability and Oversight: Prompt logs, terminal timeouts, and hallucination correction cycles show how easily the model can be steered, or how much it resists. Does the developer keep meaningful control, or does the agentic system turn into an opaque black box that nobody can really answer for? This represents the epistemic link, and this criterion is where it gets tested.

Criterion 2 → Linguistic Sovereignty: Coding journals and search mismatch logs are checked for errors, contextual failures, and semantic degradation when the system processes literary Arabic (Fusha) legal texts. The pattern of failures here is a concrete measure of how far Western-centric frontier models reproduce linguistic imperialism and algorithmic colonialism.

Criterion 3 → Market Dependency vs Public Value: Development logs and billing receipts map the ties between a civil society initiative and the capital-intensive corporate monopolies it depends on. Building a public civic tool on commercial API’s that can fail or change their pricing overnight is the paradox at the heart of this criterion, and it acts as the technological → civic link in the chain.

Criterion 4 → Information Asymmetry Mitigation: Antigravity changelogs and deployment logs track the intellectual, infrastructural, and computational labor it actually takes to turn an unsearchable data format into something usable, asking the question of whether the ‘democratization of software building’ really lets a citizen dismantle state-enforced data hoarding, or does it just look that way from the outside?

The Coding Process

Coding each friction event happens in three distinct steps: firstly, isolating the event in the logs, for instance, a five-minute backend hang, or a 402 error. Second, I categorize it by root cause: linguistic, commercial, or epistemic. Third, I code it for what it did to the agency-chain, while observing whether I had to forcibly interrupt the AI to regain oversight, or whether the system quietly cascaded into a structural compromise on its own. Every claim in the results traces back to this sequence of logged code and conversation.

Limitations of the Dataset

The dataset used for analysis has three limitations affecting how much confidence the analysis can claim:

The first is a Git/Antigravity data recovery issue encountered. During deployment, when pushing the final tool to GitHub, the process kept timing out because the repository had swollen to 13 GB, of which 4.5GB was Git history alone. Deploying the tool publicly meant having to do a ‘clean push,’ which resulted in the deletion of the .git history entirely. Therefore, the analysis relies heavily on the exported Antigravity markdown cascades rather than a continuous, commit-by-commit Git log. The cascades provide exact timestamps and verbatim prompts, but lose the granular Git history; therefore, some of the connective tissue between micro-edits is gone for good. The same infrastructural limit that left the tool architecturally incomplete also damaged the record of how it was built.

Second, the Arabic competency limitation of which I am guilty. This project involves OCR extraction and semantic embedding of complex Fusha legal texts, and I do not have the native Arabic legal literacy to independently check whether morphological nuance or specialized legal terminology survived the OCR pipeline intact. In order to work around this, I used an AI-assisted OCR comparison tool to audit the text. There is an obvious irony which should be noted: one corporate AI model auditing another corporate AI model’s handling of an underrepresented language. This isn’t just a personal limitation of mine; it is a structural feature of building for a non-Western linguistic context from the outside, and it additionally means Criterion 2’s findings on linguistic fidelity need to be read with some caution.

The third limitation concerns the temporal dynamics of frontier AI development, with the empirical build of the project taking place between January and April 2026; in the months since, the capabilities of agentic frameworks, the size of context windows, the pricing structures of commercial API’s, and rate limits have all continued to evolve, alongside the models ‘intelligence’. As Abou Ali et al. (2025) note in their own limitations, the “rapid evolution of the neural paradigm” (p. 11) means that research into agentic systems is perpetually chasing a moving target. In consequence, some of the frictions encountered in the build may have been alleviated by the release of newer, cheaper or larger models.

However, while specific technical thresholds shift over time, the structural and material constraints they reveal remain constant. For instance, an increase in context window does not resolve the opacity of how an agentic system embeds data, or a cheaper API does not eliminate the fundamental vulnerability of relying on a corporate monopoly for civic infrastructure. Therefore, while the technological capabilities may have advanced, the theoretical findings regarding market dependency, algorithmic opacity, and the infrastructural ceilings of civic tech remain structurally sound.

3.5 Dataset Scope and Scaling

The Official Lebanese Gazette’s sheer size puts real material and financial limits on the development process; the full dataset, spanning 11 years from 2014 to 2025, runs to 816 issues, 149,094 images and 14.52 GB. These are low-resolution scans of Arabic legal text, stored as static images rather than machine-readable tables, therefore requiring an additional multi-layered processing pipeline to extract the text.

At current commercial API pricing, processing a dataset this size would cost more in tokens and fees than an independent, public-interest project can absorb. In order to keep the research financially and technically viable while still putting the pipeline through its paces, I was forced to scale the dataset down to a single year: 2025. This gave me a bounded, concentrated sandbox from which to operate, providing enough to expose the structural costs and stability limits built into the technological link of the agency-chain, and to show where grassroots civic agency runs into material corporate ceilings, without burning through every resource I have.

However, that scaling comes with a real cost; restricting the corpus to 2025 means giving up the longitudinal side of the project entirely. Mapping historical trends across a decade, tracking how legislation evolves, visualizing multi-year topic densities: all these were central to the platform’s original design, and none of them can really be tested at this scale. The 2025 sandbox, however, does test the technological and epistemic links of the agency-chain: can the system extract, embed, and search complex Fusha legal text, or can’t it? The civic link is a different story altogether. At this scale, the platform can show a snapshot of civic transparency at one moment in time; a full decade-long archive with the historical accountability that comes with it is a different project.

Perhaps in closing, it is worth mentioning that the initial grandeur of the project could have only been surmised by either a domain expert, a veteran developer, or, on the contrary, an ignorant student; this ignorance I embody is in it of itself a point of data.

manuscript.title: 4. Case Study
manuscript.text:
4.1 The Lebanese Official Gazette as an Empirical Site

The Official Lebanese Gazette (Al-Jarida al Rasmiya Al-Lubnani) is the definitive legal record of the Lebanese state; it is the primary instrument for promulgating laws, decrees, and official notifications. Furthermore, it is the empirical site for this research because it concentrates the three weakest points of the agency-chain in one place: a state-enforced information asymmetry that severs epistemic agency at the source, a non-Western linguistic context that exposes the structural limits of the models trained overwhelmingly on English data, and a fragile digital infrastructure that the tech industry’s hype discourse routinely ignores.

The Gazette’s legal function is unambiguous: for a law or legislative decree to take effect, it must be published in said pages, but without meaningful access to it, a citizen cannot know the rules that govern their existence, let alone contest them. As such, any technological barrier to the Gazette is both a barrier to epistemic agency and a barrier to civic agency, two essential components of a functioning democracy. The Gazette, as it currently stands, is the right site to test whether the technological → epistemic → and civic chain holds in practice.

4.2 The Digital History: Access, Obstruction, and the Paywall

The Official Lebanese Gazette’s move from physical print to its current online form is not a story of progressive modernization; instead, it has been a history of shifting barriers. At every stage of its digital transformation, the terms of access have defined the limits of civic participation.

The Gazette was formally established in 1922 and modeled after the French Journal Officiel (المستشار في الجريدة الرسمية/the Lebanese Official Gazette - Haigazian University, n.d.). From the outset, it was a state instrument of legal promulgation, not a public resource per se. Access was institutional and administrative; the public was subject to the law, but had no direct access to its repository.

In 2005, the Presidency of the Council of Ministers put the Gazette online for the first time (Nassar, 2022), hosting raw PDF’s and static HTML copies on its portal. Nominal digital access, but the files were entirely non-searchable; users could not query documents for specific legal information, which meant epistemic agency was again not materially expanded. But the early 2010’s brought a dedicated subdomain with chronological browsing, without any semantic search or full-text search.

The closest the Gazette came to genuine epistemic access was May 2016, when Integrated Digital Systems (IDS) and the LIC launched a Central Online Database, digitizing records back to 1915, with search and filtering options (https://www.ids.com.lb, n.d.). But even then, the system was institutional and gated, and navigating it effectively required specialist legal knowledge.

The decisive turn came in 2018, when Decree No.2420 formalized the Electronic Official Gazette as a paid subscription service (اللحام, 2020). With that, the state stopped neglecting access and started to enclose it. The citizen is structurally barred from their own legal history, and the means to overcome that barrier now carries a price; the subscription was set at 550,000 LL per year, more expensive than the physical print edition (اللحام, 2020). The digital version remained non-searchable even to paying subscribers; the state effectively charged its citizens for something functionally worse than what had existed before.

In 2021, Law 233 passed, explicitly mandating free digital access to state documents (Maharat, 2025). The gap between that legal mandate and its technical reality is itself a critical data point: the state legally mandates free access while maintaining a technical architecture that prevents it is not negligence; it is actively contradictory. Civic agency was promised legislatively, but epistemic agency remains structurally blocked by the state’s own infrastructure. This active contradiction instantiates Criterion 4 and demonstrates how information asymmetry is enforced through technical friction, rather than a legal decree. Consequently, what the Lebanese Gazette’s digital presence currently amounts to is an archive frozen in place, not a public resource but a document of structural obstruction.

4.3 The Structural Context: A Critical Case

Lebanon’s digital landscape is built on a paradox: internet penetration sits at over 91% (Kemp, 2025),  yet the state’s primary legal record is nearly inaccessible. Lebanon ranks 126th in the 2024 UN E-Government Index, showing that the statistical trace of an administrative decay that connectivity figures alone cannot and do not show. The World Bank approved a 350$ million loan in 2026, with 150$ million earmarked for the Lebanon Digital Acceleration Program (World Bank Group, 2026), signaling real external pressure to modernize. But external capital does not rebuild state capacity; within this fractured infrastructure, the Gazette functions as a dead archive, being nominally digital, functionally inaccessible, and keeping citizens at arm’s length from their own legal record.

Methodologically, Lebanon is a critical case, not because it is exceptional, but because its structural frictions are so concentrated that they cannot be explained away. The tensions that could be quietly absorbed or deferred in a well-resourced Western context are, in Lebanon, load-bearing. The state has engineered an archive that is nominally digital but functionally inaccessible, testing AI’s democratizing claims in that environment; it cannot be limited to a simple theoretical critique; it must be felt through the building process, through material friction. The following chapter documents that attempt by applying the oscillating method to trace what the reconstruction of the Gazette from the outside actually involved, starting with the state’s hostile interface.

manuscript.title: 5. Results & Analysis
manuscript.text:
5.1 The State’s Hostile Interface

Positionality and the Structural Barrier

In principle, the Official Lebanese Gazette (Al Jarrida-Al-Rasmiyya Al Lubnane) should be accessible to any Lebanese Citizen online; in practice, however, accessing it requires a physical card available at a select few locations in Lebanon: the Prime Minister’s office, the Official Gazette Department, the offices of the Lebanese Electronic Book House, and LibanPost. There is no international purchase mechanism, no digital delivery, and no alternative for citizens living abroad. I am writing this from Amsterdam, as a French-Lebanese student who could not access his own country’s primary and definitive legal archive without arranging a proxy in Beirut to obtain a card on my behalf. That arrangement does not constitute a personal background, but rather the first methodological data point.

The digital transition of the Gazette did not solve the access problem for the diaspora, instead ignoring it entirely. The word “digital” is in this context doing false work. Lebanon has a diaspora of 15.4 million compared to a domestic resident population of 5.8 million, and remittances constitute 17.7% of GDP in 2024. This constituency is structurally excluded from the state’s primary legal publication by a physical activation card, a format which contradicts directly Article 18 of the Access to Information Law, which explicitly mandates free access to administrative documents; the subscription fee therefore stands in direct legal contradiction to this provision.

The Proxy Chain

In order to procure an activation card, I contacted my uncle in Beirut on January 8, 2026, initiating a proxy chain. Over the following days, documented in our WhatsApp exchanges and audio recordings, he navigated LibanPost delays and direct calls to Dar Al-Kitab Al-Elektroni.

To finalize this already strenuous process, I had to provide my email and a front-and-back picture of my Lebanese ID over WhatsApp. Submitting a photograph of my Lebanese ID to a family member to forward to a government body in order to access public documents highlights the privacy cost of accessing public information, adding a distinct dimension to the social barrier.

The state’s “digital” archive requires an analog personal network to access, and that network is not universally available. When I turned to Gemini on the 16th of January, at 18:02, to troubleshoot the portal’s ongoing errors, its response cataloged the system’s recurring failure modes such as: character confusion between 0 and O in the activation code, point-of-sale activation errors at LibanPost, and database sync delay. The final suggestion was to make a phone call to the technical support hotline, located at the Grand Serail, the Prime Minister’s office building.

January 16th: The Card Fails

On January 16, 2026, my uncle sent me pictures of the physical activation card after standing in line since 8:30 am, however, when I attempted to log in from Amsterdam using the provided credentials, the portal repeatedly returned an”incorrect” error. Having exhausted my own resources, I turned to Gemini once more. At 18:02, this would become the first turn of a recurring pattern of oscillation between technical problem-solving and critical reflection.

From a vibe-coder’s reading, an access error was handled with a technical workaround that leveraged the LLM to proceed to the next step; from the researcher’s reading, this was a system that had failed at every layer: from the portal to the card, the activation, the information, and the hotline. These failures are not bugs but features of an infrastructure designed around exclusion rather than access. The moment represents the first activation of the oscillation method.

Under Criterion 4, what was promised was digital access to public law, updated weekly, and available online. What existed, on the other hand, was a subscription gated by a physical card, available only domestically, with a non-functional portal and documented recurring failures. The gap between promise and reality precedes the AI intervention entirely. The information asymmetry this project sought to address was not created by inadequate digitization; instead, it was reproduced through the process of digitization, with new layers of obstruction added to existing ones. The service is engineered to exclude, not through a deliberately malicious design, but through the cumulative effects of decisions that function as an exclusion mechanism.

Having exhausted every official channel available to a Lebanese citizen living abroad, the intervention began not as a methodological choice but as the only remaining option.

5.2 The blind loop: Labor, anti-scraping architecture, and the cost of access.

The scene

On the 2nd of February, 2026, the activation card finally granted me access to the portal, but I quickly discovered that access to view does not necessarily mean access to use. The archive exists as individual PNG images, served page by page through a hostile JavaScript viewer. To build a searchable tool, the raw files are required locally, yet the Official Lebanese Gazette provides no bulk download button, no API, and no official data export mechanism. The state provides access to read, not access to build. Everything that follows in the build depended on solving this problem first; the data pipeline is the foundation, and if it failed, nothing else could be built.

The Six Iterations

The scraping phase unfolded chronologically across six distinct iterations, escalating from simple queries into a direct confrontation with teh portal’s anti-scraping architecture.

A first attempt at 12:17 began with a basic Python script utilizing requests and BeautifulSoup. The site, however, uses JavaScript to load its content, leaving the static scraper with an empty shell.

A second approach at 12:26 seemed like a breakthrough; the AI identified a printall endpoint (jo.pcm.gov.lb/pdfs/printall-1.php?issueId=XXXX), appearing to be a direct PDF download; however, the site lazy-loads each page individually, resulting in only the first page being downloaded.

A third iteration at 13:02 attempted to use BeautifulSoup to extract span tags with data-url attributes, but this failed entirely, returning “no pages found,” because the tags only appear after the JavaScript renders, not in the static HTML that teh scraper sees.

A fourth attempt was made at 13:35, deploying a pattern-based blind loop, incrementing page numbers (.../{year}/{issueId}/{page}.png) until hitting a 404 error. Yet the scraper inexplicably stopped at 8 pages on one issue and 4 on another, despite the issues having over a hundred pages. The server was returning fake 200 responses with tiny placeholder images below 5KB to fool scrapers.

The fifth iteration, at 13:52, marked a critical shift. I opened the browser’s developer tools myself and saw all the PNG links directly in the inspector, recognizing that the images only load after scrolling. I told the AI: “when I click inspect, I can see all the links to the png’s, so we need to find a way to download them from the console directly…” This was the oscillating method in operation: the vibe-coder hit the technical ceiling, and the researcher stepped back to look at the system itself. My own incompetence as a developer felt as though they were producing incompetence in the LLM, culminating in the AI repeatedly offering marketing-speak like renaming the code to a “Smart Multi-page scraper” rather than solving the structural issue. The solution came not from the AI, but from direct observation of the portal.

The sixth and final iteration produced the working scraper, the Blind Loop. It incorporated a size threshold check to ignore any image under 5KB, user-agent spoofing to disguise the script as a Chrome browser on Windows, and randomized jitter delays (time.sleep(random.uniform(0.3, 0.8)). It utilized lossless stitching with img2pdf to preserve OCR quality and a resume capability to skip existing downloads. The final push was marred by frustrating terminal dumps, ModuleNotFoundError’s, and pip3 environment mismatches as I shifted back to a local VSCode environment. At 14:19, the command ‘pip’ was not found, as it was tied to an older version of Python. When running Pip3, a ‘ModuleNotFoundError’ occurred nine minutes later at 14:28, failing to find the ‘requests’ module as there had been a mismatch in Python environments with the packages being installed in a different environment or path. The AI finally returned the exact commands to install the packages directly into the Homebrew Python path, but this environmental mismatch revealed a core boundary of agentic development: while the AI can almost instantly generate complex logic, the human developer is constantly left to negotiate and maintain the messy material infrastructure required to actually execute it.

What the Architecture Reveals

The government portal serves Gazette issues as individual PNG images through a JavaScript viewer; this is not a legacy technical limitation; it is instead a deliberate architectural choice, with a specific effect: to make the archive viewable but not processable. A citizen can read the Gazette page by page, but a developer cannot access it programmatically without significant reverse-engineering efforts. This distinction between read access and build access is not accidental; it is the line between consumption and agency. This echoes strongly with what Novelli et al. (2023) would identify as access without accountability; the Gazette’s current architecture enables citizens to see the information and verify it exists, but cannot interrogate it, constrain its gatekeeping, or exercise the oversight that transforms raw access into meaningful democratic capacity.

What the state calls a digital archive is simply an interface that permits reading and prevents building.

The labor costs of those six iterations constitute a finding in itself. It required prior knowledge of web scraping, familiarity with browser developer tools, the ability to read and debug Python, access to a coding AI assistant, two hours of concentrated problem-solving, a functioning laptop, and a stable, fast internet connection. The hype discourse’s claim that AI lowers barriers to capability was tested directly: the barrier was indeed lowered; a non-developer could not have built this scraper unaided before frontier AI, but it was not completely dissolved. The ‘democratization’ of software is partial, conditional, and resource-dependent; furthermore, the AI’s initial recommendation to unnecessarily entangle the project with Google Drive API’s, predatory complexity despite the promise of $300 in free credits, demonstrates an inherent vulnerability. This complexity is not just excessive; it is predatory in nature. By exploiting the non-expert developer’s reliance on the LLM’s guidance. That epistemic trust becomes a steering mechanism away from free, local solutions, toward a cloud ecosystem. The free credits being part of the same logic, by passing highly specific, culturally valuable Arabic legal data through the commercial API’s, the citizen developer inadvertently becomes an unpaid data engineer, extracting and structuring the state’s hoarded information, then feeding it back into Western models to improve their Arabic NLP capabilities at no cost. This instance is not limited to Arabic NLP but rather to the ecosystem as a whole.

Under Criterion 3, to access public legal documents, I had to rely on corporate infrastructure, disguise my identity through a user-agent spoofing, mimic human behavior with jitter delays, and reverse-engineer a system designed to prevent exactly what I was doing. The labor cost of that afternoon is not incidental; it is the metric by which the hype discourse’s claims about AI and access should be measured.

The scraper had finally worked; however, the download process itself took 4 relentless days, the literal time cost of extracting data from a system designed to prevent extraction. The data had been liberated from the portal, but 15GB of scanned PNG’s is not a searchable archive.
