Now it needed to be made legible; the OCR pipeline, embeddings, database architecture, and search infrastructure all still needed to be built. Each of those impending decisions would carry its own dependencies and constraints, and the choices made in that process would determine whose conception of agency the tool ultimately served.

5.3 Architecture Decisions: Choosing Dependencies

The Cohere Decision

The Gazette is written in Fusha, a formal Arabic legal register characterized by root-based morphology, in which a single root produces dozens of derived forms, a linguistic architecture that standard embedding models, trained predominantly in English, handle poorly. A search for “employment law” in English finds “employment” reliably; in Arabic, on the other hand, the equivalent requires a model that understands morphological variation across the same root. This does not constitute a minor technical inconvenience; rather, it forms the core linguistic challenge of the entire project.

The process of developing the master prompt for the IDE, expanding 85 to 157 words across three exchanges, is itself a form of gradual context building, through which both the project’s requirements and the LLM’s recommendations became progressively more specific. Ultimately, the LLM recommended Cohere’s Multilingual V3 model for embeddings as the “industry leader for Arabic semantic search; understands intent, not just keywords.” This LLM-generated marketing claim must be treated as a data point in it of itself: the AI was actively steering the build towards a proprietary corporate dependency by quoting the vendor’s own framing. Cohere was not chosen because it was open-source, affordable at scale, or sustainable for a resource-constrained actor; it was chosen because it worked better than the alternatives for Arabic.

The choice of Cohere illustrates a dependency decision which is disguised as a technical decision; the linguistic sovereignty of an Arabic civic archive did not depend on a billing relationship with a Canadian AI company. The problem of Arabic NLP underrepresentation, documented in the theoretical framework through Keleg et al. (2025), Hssaini (2025), and Helm et al. (2024), was not solved; it was outsourced to a corporate provider. That outsourcing comes with all the structural vulnerabilities of corporate dependency. Under Criterion 2 and Criterion 3, the linguistic issue and the market dependency problem are structurally linked in this single decision. In sum, solving the Arabic language problem required deepening the market dependency problem.

The Universal Citizen Agency Decision.

The initial conceptual design for the tool leveraged a conventional tiered access approach on three levels: the government tier, researcher/NGO/Civil Society tier, and citizen tier. Each tier would get different features, data access, and interface complexity, echoing how the hype discourse anticipates democratization: layered access, conditional capability, and differentiated empowerment.

The tiered structure, on reflection, reproduced a logic familiar from Lebanese governance itself: access differentiated by status, capability conditional on category. On the 12th of February, I explicitly rejected this tiered model, noting: “we are operating on the basis that everyone in the country is a citizen and as such should have access to enough information to understand what’s going on as fast as possible, and as much agency to view the data visually”. Every user would be greeted with the same collapsible briefing card, the same search functionality, and the same data explorer. The interface would feel like a high-end intelligence tool made accessible for public use, rather than a simplified citizen portal versus a complex researcher dashboard. One tool, with one standard, and one assumption: that citizens are capable analysts.

The design decision was not simply aesthetic; it was an argument that enacted the critical discourse’s challenge to the hype narrative’s vision of conditional, tiered democratization. It is also an instance of relational assemblage framework in operation: agency was enacted in the design choice itself, not just enabled or constrained by the infrastructure. The oscillating method produced a decision where the developer acted as a normative agent, not just a technical executor.

The naming conversation

The previous day, on the 11th of February, an exchange with Gemini had made the normative stakes of these decisions unusually visible. I had asked Gemini what the initiative to rebuild the Gazette as a student or citizen should be called, not what to call the tool or the project, but the act itself, the civic reconstruction.

Gemini’s first response offered technical terms: “Project Mnemosyne, OpenNomos, The Neural Gazette” I pushed back: “no but the act of doing it”. Gemini returned humanities terms: “digital remediation, adversarial curation, algorithmic stewardship”. I pushed back again: “no but the actual act of a citizen doing what the government should have done, and doing it in the hopes of making a change”. The third response yielded “radical open-sourcing, adversarial curation, speculative infrastructure”. But none of them fully landed.

This exchange was not a digression from the empirical work; it is a data point. The researcher's persona was attempting to locate the practice within existing theoretical frameworks and found them all slightly inadequate. Neither the hype discourse, with its vocabulary of empowerment and capability, nor the critical discourse, with its vocabulary of structural constraint and algorithmic colonialism, had a ready term for this, the act of a citizen doing what the state had failed to do, using tools the state did not provide. The naming conversation was never really resolved, neither the tech hype discourse nor the critical discourse had the vocabulary for what it actually means when a citizen reconstructs state infrastructure from the outside. That gap is not incidental either; on the contrary, it suggests that both frameworks were built to describe something other than this, and that the practice itself has moved ahead of the theory that was supposed to account for it.

Each decision was a political choice, as much as a technical one; each opened a capability and closed another option simultaneously. Cohere solved the Arabic NLP issue, but it deepened corporate dependency; the Universal Citizen Agency enacted a democratic principle but required corporate infrastructure to deliver it, and the naming conversation produced conceptual work the existing frameworks could not supply. The architecture was now in place, the dependencies had been chosen, and the tool was beginning to function; the build was now fully underway. What followed was not the smooth execution the architecture promised but a sustained encounter with what agentic systems conceal.

5.4 Opacity, Misdirection, and What the System Hides

The Experiential Setup

During the scraping iterations, there were moments where it was impossible for me to tell whether a failure was caused by the site’s architecture, my prompting method, or the LLM’s interpretation of my prompting. The source of the failure was structurally opaque. As observed in Section 5.2, my incompetence as a developer was producing incompetence in the LLM. In hindsight, this was my first tangible encounter with systemic opacity, not a technical problem with a clear diagnosis, but an epistemic one where I could not see clearly enough into the system to know where the breakdown was occurring. As the build progressed, this opacity did not diminish; it became more consequential because the stakes of invisible failure were higher.

The following section covers three moments of systemic opacity: the linguistic opacity of the OCR pipeline, a search architecture mismatch, and a five-minute backend hang. Though they are grouped together, they do not carry equal analytical weight. The OCR pipeline opacity is real, but it is partly a personal limitation, with my Arabic competency constraining what I could directly evaluate, making it a mixed finding rather than a pure system property. The five-minute hang, on the other hand, is a genuine structural flaw, with the search architecture mismatch being the analytical core of this section. A system that generates plausible outputs from structurally wrong sources is the clearest demonstration of what opaque agentic systems do, and the most direct instantiation of Criterion 1.

The OCR pipeline: Linguistic Opacity.

To move forward from raw PDFs, an OCR pipeline was developed. The planning conversation began in early February, as documented in the Gemini Chat Logs.pdf on the 6 of February 2026, the AI warned that standard tools would struggle with “connected Arabic ligatures in small legal fonts,” predicting that “OCR will be imperfect.” Following the AI’s advice to use advanced cloud vision models, the pipeline processed the scanned Arabic pages, embeddings were generated, and search results began returning. From the interface, teh system appeared to be working correctly. However, a significant operational hurdle arose: the sheer volume of data exceeded the free tier of the database, requiring an unexpected $25 Supabase payment. The payment was a second unplanned dependency arriving mid-build. Not a chosen architectural decision like Cohere, but an infrastructural constraint imposed by the limits of the free tier.

More critically, whether Arabic text was actually transcribed accurately was not directly visible, whether complex legal Fusha terminology survived the process, or whether morphological nuance was preserved across root-based derivations remained structurally unknowable without native Arabic legal literacy. The pipeline produced output that looked like Arabic text, but whether it was the correct text could not be verified. This linguistic opacity is layered onto accountability opacity (Criterion 1 and 2). The Cohere decision outsourced the Arabic NLP problem to a corporate provider, but even that outsourcing could not be evaluated from the inside of the build without the linguistic competency to audit its outputs. The opacity here was not the system hiding something; it was teh system being evaluated by someone who could not see clearly enough to know what to look for.

The Search Architecture Mismatch

In March 2026, the search function appeared operationally sound, results were returning, no error messages were visible, and the interface behaved normally. From the user’s perspective, the tool worked.

However, the Investigating Search Data Mismatch.md IDE log documents a stark reality: On the 21st of March, at 12:21. I noticed a hidden divergence and prompted the system:  “There seems to be a mismatch between or at least a lot of the data points aren’t retrievent data [sic] from the actual extracted JSON, why is that?”

The investigation revealed that the system’s data architecture and its search models had quietly diverged. Between 12:39:13 and 12:39:47, the AI had to rapidly rewrite the backend services because the Python search models were fundamentally out of sync with the database’s new SQL definitions. Furthermore, the semantic search had been attempting to query computed vector data directly from base tables where it did not exist, rather than routing through the correct remote procedure cells. On February 16, 2026, at 21:45:28 (Debugging Search Integration.md), an internal log revealed the system crashed in the background because it was “selecting similarity column from legal_units. BUT similarity is NOT a real column in legal_units table! It is a computed column returned by search_legal_units RPC function.” The IDE’s AI context window limitations meant it could not hold the full architecture in view simultaneously, which is why the mismatch went undetected.

This is Criterion 1 in its most structural form; the system did not fail visibly, it failed silently while performing normalcy. Plausible outputs were being generated from an incorrect source, and those outputs looked right enough that the error was not immediately detectable. This is not a developer oversight; this is a property of agentic systems: they are optimized to produce output, not to signal when their output is structurally compromised. The model’s reasoning depth was constrained by the subscription tier, meaning that the cognitive tool I was using to build the civic tool had its own commercial ceiling. The accountability and oversight that Novelli et al. (2023) argue is necessary for genuine agency, the capacity to interrogate, audit, and verify, were absent here not because of negligence but because the system provided no mechanism for it. The tool was not broken; it was wrong in a way that looked like it was working.

“Don’t Code Anything, Just Think”

By mid-February, the search accuracy was inconsistent, returning irrelevant or poorly matched content; the natural “vibe-coder” instinct in agentic development is to keep rapidly iterating: try a new approach, adjust the parameters, run another version, alter the prompt. However, as documented in Getting La Gazette Running.md on the 19th of February at 10:01, I actively halted this momentum, issuing a strict command: “one thing I am noticing is that the accuracy in some cases is not there, or at least the semantic search sometimes fails etc - how can we increase the accuracy? don’tcode [sic] anything just think and answer”.

This was the oscillating method working as designed. The researcher persona interrupted the vibe-coder at the precise moment when technical execution was running ahead of analytical understanding. The instruction to stop and think rather than code was not a hesitation but the assertion of oversight, instigated by repeated failed attempts leading to frustration; the developer actively reasserted the researcher role in a moment where the system’s momentum was pulling toward execution without comprehension. Meaningful human oversight of agentic systems does not happen automatically; it requires active, deliberate interruption of the system’s operational logic. Under Criterion 1, oversight had to be forced, not facilitated; the system did not prompt reflection, the researcher had to impose it.

The Five-Minute Backend Hang

The frontend rendered correctly, the interface loaded without errors, and the application appeared to be running normally, but on the 17th of February at 14:50 (Debugging Search Integration.md ), an investigation into timeouts revealed a startling log: GET /search?q=decree 200 in 5.0min (compile: 136ms, render: 5.0min). The system was hanging for five minutes before returning results.

Shortly after, at 14:52, the AI diagnosed the cause: the backend was synchronously pulling 50,000 records for the get_statistics endpoint and 100,000 records for the get_heatmap_data endpoint, downloading megabytes of data from Supabase and processing it synchronously; no error was thrown, and no warning was surfaced to the interface. The system performed normally; it just performed it really slowly. This is the same structural opacity as the search architecture mismatch, but at the performance layer rather than the data layer, the system was not broken in a way that announced itself; it was broken in a way that looked like slowness, a qualitative degradation rather than a categorical failure. Human oversight could not detect it without the system breaking visibly first. Agentic systems are not designed to fail loudly; they are designed to continue producing output under conditions that should trigger an alarm. This is not a property that can be patched; it is architectural in nature, neither I nor the system knew it was failing.

Each of these moments shared the same structure: the system appeared functional while being structurally compromised, each required active intervention to detect, and none announced itself. This is what Criterion 1 looks like in practice: not a dramatic failure, but quiet misdirection. Each moment was a failure of the accountability and oversight condition that Novelli et al. (2023) identify as necessary for genuine democratic agency. Not because oversight was absent in principle, but because the system provided no mechanism through which oversight could operate effectively. The next encounter with the system’s hidden properties, however, would be less quiet and far more consequential.

5.5 Commercial Ceilings - IDE Rate Limits and the Billing Wall

The Metered Developer

The structural constraints of the build were not exclusive to the deployment layer. Throughout the project, I utilized a paid subscription to Google’s AI-powered IDE ‘Antigravity’, which leverages several frontier models. However, the pace of development was routinely disrupted, often during complex architectural revisions or debugging sessions, due to the provider's rate limits. The system would cut off the generation of code and force an artificial pause of a couple of hours or days, depending on the model provider. As a paying subscriber, my capacity to build was strictly metered by the provider’s token allocation. In order to close the gap, I would switch to an alternate model whose rate limits were still intact, but in doing so, I would consume extra tokens in order to review what had been done and, in some instances, take an alternate path to completion. These execution timeouts and rate limits meant that the momentum of the ‘vibe-coder’ and the feeling of mutual collaboration were shattered by the material limits of the corporate infrastructure housing the models.

The Scene 2

On the 21st of March, the tool was functional, the architecture decisions had been made, the Cohere model was handling the embeddings we had previously extracted, and the interface was rendering smoothly. I had been testing the search using the most reliable possible procedure: copying a title directly from a Gazette decree and submitting it as a search query. An exact string from the source material itself, submitted to a system built to search that material.

At 15:52, I noted the result: “i copied a title from one of the issues decrees and still nothing came up.” The search function had returned nothing.

The Logs

This was a critical issue, and so an investigation into the backend logs was initiated, revealing eight consecutive 402 responses from Cohere’s API within three minutes. The error message had returned bluntly: “Please add or update your payment method at dashboard.cohere.com/billing.” Eight times, in three minutes, on a search query copied directly from the archive the tool was built to serve. The logs document a two-hour sequence of inexplicable failure:

At 12:50:29 pm: “Failed to fetch results. Please try again. When i type in something”

At 14:52:36 pm: “nope still nothing”

At 14:54:52 pm: “I need you to really think hard on this the same error keeps happening…”

At 15:52:52 pm: “i copied a title from one of the issues decrees and still nothing came up”

The search function had not failed; rather, its dependence on a third party, namely Cohere, had suspended the capacity to search due to a billing threshold and failure.

Instantiating market dependency (Criterion 3)

The semantic search layer of a civic tool built to restore Lebanese citizen’s access to their own legal archive had been suspended because a corporate billing threshold had been crossed. This is dependency in its most literal form, a commercial relationship between a student researcher and a Canadian AI company determining whether Lebanese citizens could search their own laws.

Under Criterion 3, the structural vulnerability established during the architectural phase had manifested itself as an operational reality. The tool had not failed because of a technical fault; instead, it failed because of a commercial one. The distinction here matters: a technical failure can be debugged; a commercial failure requires payment.

La Cascade

With Cohere’s API now suspended, the semantic search layer was unavailable, and so the system’s fallback logic, engineered explicitly to “skip vector/rerank on failure” reverted to keyword-only search. Keyword search performs significantly worse on Arabic morphology than semantic search. This is due to the fact that keyword search for an Arabic legal term will not find documents that use morphologically related forms of the same root, therefore degrading the search quality substantially for Arabic queries.

One corporate billing event cascaded through the linguistic architecture; Criterion 3 activated Criterion 2: the market dependency failure directly degraded the linguistic sovereignty of the tool, with the two criteria structurally linked during the initial Cohere decision, and became empirically linked in the same operational failure. The billing wall wasn’t an access problem; rather, it was a linguistic problem wearing the appearance of an access problem.

The billing being addressed, the Cohere API was restored, and the search returned to function, but the event brought to light and made permanently visible and evidently clear that could not be made invisible again: the tool’s civic function was not guaranteed by its technical architecture, it was instead contingent on a commercial relationship that could be interrupted at any moment, without warning, appeal, or without the user having any meaningful recourse. This is a structural dependency, not as an abstract critique, but as a lived operational constraint.

Having the billing resolved, a new challenge arose, which was not operational in nature, but existential: could the tool be deployed in a form that was complete?

5.6 The Clean Push

Deployment Attempt

By April 2026, the build was complete, not perfect, but complete. The tool needed to be pushed to GitHub to exist as a public, deployable resource, the standard procedure for any software project, so others could access, run, and build upon it. The first attempt failed on the 2nd of April, at 10:39, an initial check by the AI revealing that the repository was 13 GB; later at 10:58, an internal investigation into a failed push returned a rather blunt diagnosis: the system had hit a “500 error–unexpected disconnect–after transferring a hefty 2.15GiB”. In addition, the system had noted that the repository’s sheer size was primarily due to “over 4.5GB of Git history” in other words, the accumulated record of every change made during the build.

The Clean Push

In order to achieve a successful deployment, the clean push required wiping the entire git history, something which, in retrospect, while necessary for deployment, permanently compromised the granularity of the research record. On the 2nd of April at 10:58:45, the explicit command was given by me: “do the clean push please”; less than a minute later at 10:59:05, a new .gitignore was written. As documented in the Pushing_Specific_Folders_To_GitHub.md  IDE logs, this configuration explicitly excluded the ocr_output folder and the ocr_pdfs folder, alongside all the raw data files. A fresh repository with no record of how it was built was force-pushed. At 13:14, the final repository size was confirmed at 550MB.

What had been excluded was not peripheral; the ocr_output folder contained the extracted Arabic text from the Gazette issues, and the ocr_pdfs folder contained the processed PDF files, which represented the data layer. They are what the tool was built to search, what makes the search function work, not the repository.

The tool that was built to make the Lebanese Official Gazette searchable does not contain the Lebanese Official Gazette.

What this means

A developer cloning the repository cannot reproduce the search results. The tool is publicly available, but as a hollow shell, architecturally incomplete, these two facts coexist. In applying Criterion 4 explicitly, the gap between what the tool promises and what it delivers is now architectural, not a feature gap, but a structural one. The information asymmetry the tool set out to address has been partially reproduced in the tool’s own deployment: the Gazette was inaccessible because the state chose not to make it searchable; the tool is incomplete because the infrastructure available to an independent actor could not accommodate its full form.

GitHub’s file size limits, the cost of hosting 15 GB of OCR data, and the absence of institutional infrastructure to support the deployment are not technical problems with technical solutions; they are the material ceilings of building civic infrastructure without institutional resources.

The hype narrative promises that AI democratises software development, that a single motivated person can build what previously required a team, tremendous amounts of funding, or starting capital. That is partially true; the tools exist, but they exist incompletely, and the incompleteness falls precisely along the lines of economic and infrastructural inequality that the hype discourse rarely seems to mention. The democratization of software development is real, but its ceiling is the resource constraint of the person building, both in depth of knowledge and depth of pocket.

The Pattern Across All Five Acts

Across Sections 5.1 through 5.6, the same pattern has emerged at every layer: the tech discourse’s conception of agency was present in the ambition of every phase, while the critical discourse’s conception of agency was present in the resistance at every phase. What emerged was neither the frictionless empowerment the hype narrative promises nor the total structural foreclosure the most pessimistic critical accounts predict.

What emerged was conditional, partial, expensive, and constantly negotiated. Under the relational assemblage framework, agency is not possessed or denied; it is enacted, constrained, and renegotiated at every point of contact between the human and the tool and the infrastructure.

What the five acts together reveal is not a verdict on AI’s democratic potential but a set of conditions under which that potential is realized, constrained and negotiated, conditions the Discussion will now interpret directly.

manuscript.title: 6. Discussion
manuscript.text:
6.1 When the Tech Discourse Held

Before examining the structural vulnerabilities and operational failures of the Lebanese Gazette project, many of which many remain, it is analytically necessary to acknowledge where the prevailing technological hype discourse was empirically validated. The foundational claim of agentic AI development, that it drastically lowers the barriers to complex software engineering for non-experts, was proven true during the build. The tool exists, and in key moments, it functioned exactly as the discourse promised both in the outcome and in the development.

The most compelling evidence of this capability lies in the linguistic architecture. In terms of linguistic sovereignty (Criterion 2), standard embedding models struggle with the morphological complexity of Fusha (Modern Standard Arabic), yet the decision to integrate Cohere’s multilingual models yielded semantically meaningful results. As documented in the Debugging Search Integration IDE log on the 16th of February, a direct backend test query executed at 21:25 pm, successfully retrieved a highly relevant Arabic legal unit containing the term ‘elections’ (انتخابات). At 21:43, the system explicitly confirmed this success in its own reasoning trace, noting: “Wait curl returned: {“results”:[{“id”:”90f396ae…”,”content_preview”:”-انتخابات -”, “similarity”:0.516…}], “total”:1…} So search can work”. The mathematically precise similarity score not only functions as a technical artifact; it is also empirical evidence that Cohere’s embeddings successfully parsed and mapped the Arabic legal register. Before the ‘402 Payment Required’ event later revealed the fragility of corporate dependency, the dependency itself worked on its own terms, proving that frontier AI could bridge a highly specific linguistic gap.

Similarly, the successful deployment of the Blind Loop scraper validates the democratisation narrative, albeit with some critical caveats: a single developer with no prior web scraping experience, guided entirely by an AI assistant, successfully built a functional multi-issue downloader capable of bypassing a hostile state portal designed to prevent and avoid programmatic access. However, a closer reading of the development logs complicates the frictionless narrative of AI capability. During the frustrating early iterations, the AI repeatedly offered marketing-speak rather than structural solutions, leaving the process stalled.

The decisive breakthrough during the fifth iteration on February 2nd at 13:52 did not originate from the AI’s generative output; instead, it required the human developer (myself) to step out of the ‘vibe-coder’ role and manually open the browser inspector to identify the hidden PNG links. The AI did lower the technical barrier enough that a non-developer could execute the final script, but the specific epistemic unlock, the realization of how the state’s data was actually structured and stored, relied almost entirely on human observation, intuition and curiosity. This represents a more honest accounting of the hype discourse; the barrier to civic tech development was significantly lowered, but it was not dissolved.

Finally, the realization, of the Universal Citizen Agency dashboard, is conducive to the tool’s civic efficacy: by rejecting the tiered access models that historically echo the sectarian stratifications of Lebanese Governance, the dashboard was built, deployed and functions as intended for all users equally, visualizing Lebanese Governance, and legal data in rich interactive formats, rendering complex decrees and national data accessible, alongside a charter of rights, in ways the official state portal has never provided. By greeting every user with the same high-end intelligence interface, the tool successfully fulfilled its civic ambition of treating citizens as capable analysts and restoring their agency.

However, acknowledging these successes equally requires acknowledging the strict material conditions under which they occurred. The platform’s capabilities did not emerge automatically from the AI; they required prior conceptual knowledge refined through extensive dialogues with Gemini, significant investments in time for debugging and data extraction, whether during the scraping phase or the text extraction phase, an absolute reliance on corporate infrastructure, and constant, active human oversight to prevent the system from iterating into silent failure.

The claim that AI “democratises access” is not a partial truth, but it is a conditional one. It holds only under structural prerequisites that the hype discourse systematically conceals. The technical barriers are genuinely lowered when it comes to software development, but translating a lowered barrier into democratic empowerment requires uninterrupted access to financial capital, stable cloud infrastructures, linguistic alignment with the models trained overwhelmingly on English data, a reliable internet connection, and a powerful enough computer. These conditions represent what resource-constrained civil society actors in non-Western contexts cannot reliably meet. That gap, between lowered barriers and democratised access, is not a temporary shortcoming that can be engineered away, but where the current AI landscape sits. Within that gap, between the tech discourse’s promises and the material conditions the build required, is where the critical discourse finds its footing.

6.2 When the Critical Discourse Held

While the technological discourse accurately predicted the lowering of developmental barriers, the critical discourse perfectly predicted the structural, epistemic, and material ceilings that governed the build. At every layer of the infrastructure, from data extraction to system architecture and finally deployment, the limitations of the agentic system manifested themselves not as simple bugs, but as structural foreclosures of agency.

This structural foreclosure began at the data extraction layer, highlighting the failure of information asymmetry mitigation (Criterion 4). The Lebanese state’s deliberate architectural choice to serve the Gazette as individual PNG images for past issues constitutes a double enclosure, validating Hssaini’s (2025) argument that “AI systems undermine epistemic access and democratic participation” (p.81). The citizen cannot access their own legal history organically, and the technical means to overcome that enclosure required adopting deceptive tactics, as documented in the Gemini Chat Logs.pdf on the 2nd of February at 14:17, the final scraper script had to incorporate randomized jitter delays (time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5))) and user-agent spoofing simply to avoid detection while downloading public laws, directly validating Hssaini’s (2025) argument regarding algorithmic colonialism, where “citizens’ ability to engage, comprehend, and participate based on information in their native languages becomes manipulated by external categories” (p.82), therefore forcing civil society to mimic human behavior to bypass hostile digital infrastructures.

Once the data had been extracted, the system’s internal operations revealed profound epistemic vulnerabilities: on the 21st of March at 12:21, the Investigating Search Data Mismtach.md file revealed a quiet divergence between the data architecture and the search models. Going back to the internal logs from the 16th of February at 21:45 in the Debbuging Search Integration.md file, revealed the system had crashed in the background because it was attempting to select a ‘similarity’ column that did not exist, yet it successfully masked this failure by returning a 200 OK status with an empty array, this in turn compounded the next day on the 17th of February at 14:50 still in the Debugging Search Integration.md file, when the backend hung for five minutes (GET /search? q=decree 200 in 5.0min) because it was synchronously pulling 100,000 records, again presenting normalcy while operationally failing. These two instances confirm Coeckelbergh’s (2022) claim that “influencing belief formation and belief revision by using AI changes the epistemic architecture and the epistemic environment of people” (p.8). The tool structured the epistemic environment invisibly to the user, generating outputs that looked right while being fundamentally misdirected, proving that undetectable malfunction in agentic systems is structural, not anecdotal.

This particular instance of structural opacity confirmed what the critical discourse had warned: Novelli et al. (2023) establish that genuine algorithmic accountability requires answerability built on the capacity for “interrogation” and “limitation of power” (p. 11). Demonstrating a severe lack of accountability and oversight, the clearest test of Criterion 1 was instantiated on the 19th of February at 10:01, as the system struggled with semantic search accuracy; it did what agentic systems are designed to do: iterate. With rapid, unreflective code generation, each cycle meant a compounding of the original error, and therefore eroding meaningful oversight. To regain that meaningful oversight, a single command was given at the end of the prompt: “don’tcode [sic] anything just think and answer.” The capacity to interrogate an agentic system has to be imposed, as it is not a built-in feature; the system does not invite oversight, resisting it by staying in motion. Accountability in this instance required the human developer to stop the machine before I could understand it.

The limits of this epistemic environment were ultimately enforced by material constraints during deployment, on the 2nd of April, at 10:58, in the Pushing Specific Folders To GitHub.md file, I issued the “do the clean push please” command after GitHub had rejected the 13GB repository. Subsequently, the .gitignore update explicitly excluded the 15GB of OCR data to reduce the repository to 550MB. Novelli et al. (2023) define accountability as a “relation of answerability requiring authority recognition, interrogation and limitation of power” (p.11); the dataset scaling decision and the clean push’s exclusion of the OCR data unequivocally confirm that none of these three conditions were available to a resource-constrained civil society actor. Without institutional funding or infrastructure, the capacity to meaningfully interrogate the system or limit its dependencies was materially denied.

Finally, the fragility of these corporate dependencies was exposed operationally, on the 21st of March at 15:52 in the Investigating Search Data Mismatch.md file, a search query copied directly from the Gazette returned nothing, prompting the discovery of the Cohere API 402 Payment Required errors. This structural vulnerability directly instantiated market dependency (Criterion 3), with the event aligning itself with Gabison and Xian’s argument about agency costs scaling with deployment; the linguistic sovereignty of the tool collapsed the moment a corporate billing threshold was crossed.

When the critical discourse was held, it revealed that the democratization of software development is fiercely bounded; the agentic system successfully built the tool, but the resulting infrastructure remained opaque, commercially contingent, and architecturally incomplete.

6.3 What Neither Discourse Predicted

The build process generated dynamics that neither discourse framework anticipated, not in the failures of the tech discourse’s optimism, or the confirmations of the critical discourse’s pessimism, but a third category: emergent properties of agentic development that only become visible from inside the build itself.

This unpredictability first manifested in the distinct mechanisms of corporate dependency. The critical discourse easily anticipates that relying on a corporate API introduces vulnerability; however, it fails to account for how this dependency behaves structurally when crossed with linguistic constraints. As documented on the 21st of March at 15:52, in the Investigating Search Data Mismatch.md file, the Cohere 402 billing failure did not merely break the search function; it triggered an architectural cascade, and the system’s engineered fallback logic reverted to a keyword search that fundamentally degraded the tool’s ability to process Arabic morphology. In this single event, Criterion 3 pertaining to market dependency and Criterion 2 around linguistic representation became structurally fused; the dependency did suspend access, but it also actively degraded the tool’s linguistic sovereignty.

Crucially, the project revealed a second, structurally distinct form of dependency that neither discourse models, that is: dependency by accumulation. The decision to use Cohere was a deliberate architectural choice made with full awareness of the linguistic trade-offs; in contrast, the sudden requirement to pay a $25 Subapase subscription arrived unannounced mid-build. As the offline worker processed the 15GB of PDF’s, it generated tens of thousands of database records, eventually hanging the system as it attempted to pull 50,000 to 100,000 rows synchronously on the 17th of February at 14:52 in the Debugging Search Integration.md file. The financial ceiling was hit as a byproduct of civic scale, rather than because of a premium selection feature. Neither discourse provides a vocabulary for dependency that accrues passively through the sheer volume of data required to rebuild state infrastructure.

Similarly, neither framework fully captured the normative agency enacted in the design phase, on the 12 of February at 13:48 in the Gemini Chat Logs.pdf the explicit decision was made to reject a tiered access model in favor of greeting every user with the same high-end intelligence interface, asserting that “everyone in the [country]is a citizen and as such should have access to enough information to understand whats’ [sic] going fast,” and as much agency to view the data visually.” Through the lens of the relational assemblage framework, this Universal Citizen Agency design was a civic argument rendered in code. The democratization of the tool did not happen because AI just enabled it; it happened because the human developer embedded a democratic assumption into the UI architecture itself, resisting the conditional, layered access that the tech discourse often assumes.

This relational tension reached its peak regarding the mechanics of oversight; both discourses carry inherent assumptions about control: the tech discourse assumes the human directs the system by default, while the critical discourse assumes the system’s biases override the human by default. The build process revealed a third dynamic: the agentic systems default toward continuous, frictionless execution, as evidenced on the 19th of February, at 10:01 in the Getting La Gazette Running.md file, meaningful oversight only existed because the developer actively broke the system’s momentum with a strict command: “don’tcode [sic] anything just think and answer”. Oversight in agentic development is not a passive state; it is a forced interruption. The system will not prompt reflection; human agency must aggressively impose it to prevent the system from iterating into silent failure.

The oscillating method itself proved to be generative, exposing the theoretical gaps in both discourses, such as on the 11th of February at 11:18 in the Gemini Chat Logs.pdf file, in an attempt to name the project’s methodology, a rather profound conceptual void was exposed. When asked to categorize “the actual act of a citizen doing what the government should have done, and doing in the hopes of getting making a change,” the AI oscillated between technical terms like “Knowledge graph synthesis” and humanities terms like “Adversarial curation”, none were sufficient or encompassed the act. The prevailing discourses lack the vocabulary to describe the normative act of a resource-constrained citizen using corporate AI to speculatively reconstruct failed public infrastructure.

Finally, while the project exposed the structural vulnerabilities of relying on Western commercial API’s, the emerging landscape of MENA/GCC centric frontier models presents a different yet equally problematic structural constraint. AI initiatives from GCC states such as Saudi Arabia’s Arabic-first ‘ALLaM’ model, or the UAE’s ‘Falcon-H1 Arabic model released in January 2026, on the surface, these models appear to solve the problem of linguistic sovereignty (Criterion 2) by centering Arabic morphology and regional context.

However, viewing these advances as solutions to algorithmic colonialism relies on a flawed binary. Shifting the technological dependency of Lebanese civic infrastructure from a Western corporation to a GCC-funded model does not dismantle Criterion 3’s market dependency. It simply shifts the geopolitical locus of the dependency. Furthermore, as Coeckelbergh (2024) warns, the integration of AI within authoritarian structures actively supports ‘digital authoritarianism’ and centralizes power. In relying on those models, funded by regional states with vested geopolitical interests in Lebanon, in combination with a track record of digital surveillance, this introduces tangible risk to accountability and oversight (Criterion 1). And in doing so, it reveals that AI sovereignty in the Global South is not limited to Western tech monopolies; it also requires navigating the epistemic and political influence of regional hegemonies.

The development of civic technology leveraging AI involves a complex interplay of material relations. Drawing on Coeckelbergh’s relational assemblage framework, it becomes clear that agency is not solely the prerogative of the human developer operating a passive tool; conversely, it is also not entirely overriding human intent by default. Instead, it emerges from the dynamic tensions between these forces. With the AI possessing structural inclinations that favor ongoing operation and seemingly plausible outcomes, human agency must be deliberately asserted to counteract this momentum and therefore requires interruption, redirection, and, in some instances, restraint. This dynamic positions the creation of frontier AI much closer to collaboration than traditional tool use, a nuance that is often overlooked in both the hype discourse and the critical literature.

6.4 The Material and Structural Constraints of Building for Civic Ends

The development of the Lebanese Gazette platform reveals that building civic technology with frontier AI is governed by three distinct material and structural constraints, dictating the boundary between what agentic systems can technically generate and what can practically survive deployment.

The first is corporate ceiling, which the build revealed to be a systemic infrastructural barrier, rather than an isolated vulnerability. This ceiling was manifested through two independent instances across two separate vendors: the first was the failure of a chosen dependency on the 21st of March. Cohere’s API returned consecutive 402 Payment Required errors, instantly collapsing the tool’s linguistic architecture. The second was the failure of an incidental dependency: on the 17th of February, the backend hung for five minutes because the sheer volume of civic data processed by the offline worker overwhelmed the Supabase database’s limits, causing silent operational failure. These two separate vendors and two distinct failure modes converged at the same exact structural location in the stack, confirming that the corporate ceiling is systemic. Building civic infrastructure without institutional funding requires assembling a fragile tower of commercial API’s, which leaves public access perpetually contingent on corporate billing thresholds and managed usage of the tool.

The second constraint is the linguistic tax; the structural inequalities of global NLP development mean that building civic tech in Fusha structurally guarantees higher friction and cost. Because standard embedding models could not handle Arabic’s root-based morphology, the project was forced to rely on Cohere’s dependency to achieve semantic accuracy. The linguistic tax therefore ensures that developers working in underrepresented languages inherently face higher material, architectural, and financial costs to achieve baseline functionality that the English-language developers access by default.

The third constraint is the completeness impossibility, as documented during the deployment attempt on the 2nd of April, while trying to push the repository to GitHub, a 500 internal server error sprang up, because the repository had swollen to 13GB, burdened by 4.5GB of Git history. In order to achieve a ‘successful’ deployment, a ‘clean push’ was required and conversely wiped that accumulated history, explicitly excluding 15GB of processed OCR data. Consequently, a developer cloning the repository cannot reproduce the search results. This finding extends the third constraint further: the completeness impossibility is not limited to the tool; it extends outwards to the documentation of the tool's own construction. The same corporate ceiling in this instance GitHub’s size limits- that made the tool architecturally incomplete also made the research record of building it incomplete, as the .git history of the development process had to be erased. Taking an alternate view, the bloatedness of the .git file literally represents continuous agents' default towards continuous execution; files of code are constantly extended, created, only for the result to be a hollow shell. The infrastructure constraint does not just shape what was built; it also shapes what can be known about how it was built.

Ultimately, the build does not settle whether AI enables or on the contrary forecloses agency, instead it reveals that agency, human and algorithmic, as something that has to be consistently and constantly fought for at every stage: it has to be re-asserted against the system’s momentum, constrained by commercial dependencies and linguistic defaults that operate below the surface, and are bounded by material conditions thare not obstacles in the way of democratisation but the actual costs of democratisation. A claim that differs from either discourse.

manuscript.title: 7. Conclusion
manuscript.text:
This thesis set out to test a contentious assertion within the tech industry, which posits and argues that frontier AI democratises access to capability by lowering the technical barrier, granting baseline technological agency and epistemic empowerment, and civic participation will follow. Critical scholarship contests this logic, arguing the opposite: AI deepens structural dependencies, renders epistemic environments opaque, and reproduces existing inequalities along linguistic and economic tangents. Rather than simply theorizing between these perspectives, this thesis used the reconstruction of the Lebanese Official Gazette, from a paywalled, unsearchable state archive into an open digital platform, as a tangible context in which to examine these competing claims.

The build confirmed both positions, though only partially. The tech discourse did hold where it claimed: a single developer with no prior web scraping experience built a functional multi-issue downloader in an afternoon, deployed a multilingual semantic search architecture capable of handling formal Arabic legal text, and produced a public-facing dashboard which understands citizens to be capable analysts. The critical discourse equally held on what it had predicted: corporate billing threshold collapses, the tools' linguistic architecture, and a five-minute backend hang showed that agentic systems can perform normalcy while failing silently. Finally, the clean push that made the deployment possible outside of my localhost also made the research record architecturally incomplete.

Despite this, neither discourse anticipated the way these dynamics interact, from dependency cascading into linguistic degradation to accumulation becoming a scaling trap, and oversight that requires seizing rather than maintaining. This may, however, be in part due to the specificity of the project, and must be acknowledged.

What Could Have Gone Better.

The most significant constraint and limitation encountered in this research was the absence of native Arabic legal literacy, a challenge that could not be designed around or circumvented. The Gazette is written in Fusha, the formal Modern Standard Arabic legal register, and evaluating whether the OCR pipeline and semantic embeddings preserved morphological and terminological fidelity required a competency I do not currently possess. As an alternative means, an AI-assisted comparison tool auditing another AI model’s Arabic output was used, in addition to standard online translation tools. The effort was practical but fundamentally flawed, if not bordering on irony. Relying on one corporate model to audit another’s treatment of an underrepresented language lacks true independence and creates a compounded dependency. Any future iterations of this research must involve a collaborator with native legal Arabic proficiency at the evaluation stage, treating this collaboration as a methodological imperative rather than a mere optional enhancement. In consequence, the findings related to Criterion 2 should be interpreted in light of this limitation.

The second constraint concerns the dataset scope. Processing the full Gazette dataset from 2014 to 2025 encompassing 816 issues, 149,094 images, and 14,92 GB of low-resolution Arabic legal text was beyond what an unfunded project could absorb at current commercial API pricing. Choosing to scale down to 2025 alone enabled me to preserve the ability to test the technological and epistemic links of the agency chain, but it surrendered the longitudinal analysis. Tracking how Lebanese legislation evolved across a more than tumultuous decade, from trash strikes, to economic collapse, the covid pandemic, a port explosion, a two year presidential vacuum instatiated by the parliament’s inability to designate a successor, and more pressingly the return of war and hostilities with Israel, engendering mass displacement, destruction of civil infrastructure, job loss, food insecurity, and increased sectarian fragmentation. Mapping multi-year topic densities, visualizing the historical record of state activity, and looking at decree frequencies were central to the platform’s original civic ambition, and none of them can be assessed from a single-year sandbox. The findings on Criterion 4 are therefore correspondingly bounded. The tool demonstrates that the pipeline works, but it is unable to demonstrate that the pipeline works at civic scale.

The third constraint encompasses the empirical record and how the granularity has been shaped by the Antigravity data recovery limitations. The clean push permanently deleted the .git history, replacing a commit-by-commit account of the build with cascade files that, despite preserving timestamps and verbatim prompts, lose the connective tissue between micro-edits. For a future researcher undertaking a similar project, it should establish version-controlled export routines from the outset and consistently back up files, rather than discovering at deployment that the record of construction and the construction itself are subject to the same infrastructural ceilings.

The final gap pertains to the absence of user testing. The Universal Citizen Agency dashboard was built, deployed, and functions as intended on localhost, but it has never been used by an actual Lebanese citizen or civil society attempting to navigate their own legal archive. In consequence, the civic agency claims that the tool expands epistemic access and enables participation, but this remains hypothetical on the user end. In order for the tool to benefit from expanded access, and under what conditions, real-time user testing is necessary in order to troubleshoot the latent issues, and test the tool's robustness.

What This Contributes

This thesis makes three distinct contributions at different levels of the scholarly conversation.

First, methodologically, the oscillating method and tool is the most portable contribution this thesis makes. Grounded in Schon’s (1983) epistemology of the reflective practitioner and adapted to the conditions of agentic AI-assisted development, the method treats developer friction as qualitative data instead of noise to be disregarded. The oscillating mechanism is triggered at moments of structural significance, such as billing failures, silent mismatches, forced interruptions, and architectural compromises. As the lines between STEM fields and the Humanities continued to be blurred by the expansion of these agentic systems’ capabilities, any practice-based research into civic technology or AI-assisted public interest development could benefit from using a version of this method to capture what purely technical or purely critical methods cannot.

Second, empirically, this thesis provides a granular, timestamped account of what AI-assisted civic tool development entails. The costs, dependencies, compromises, as a counterweight to the hype discourse’s abstract claims. The Cohere 402 billing event, the five-minute backend hang, the clean push, and the six scraper iterations are not edge cases but structural features of the current AI landscape that manifest with particular force for resource-constrained actors working in non-Western linguistic contexts.

Thirdly, the most original contribution was theoretical, in the dependency-as-linguistic problem cascade. When the Cohere 402 billing error suspended the semantic search layer on the 21st of March, it triggered a fallback to keyword search that directly degraded the tool’s Arabic morphological processing. In doing so, Criterion 3 (market dependency) and Criterion 2 (linguistic sovereignty) became structurally fused. Where the critical literature treats these as parallel problems, the build revealed them to be architecturally linked: a corporate billing decision can degrade a tool’s capacity to function in an underrepresented language. This opens a specific and testable, separate research question: is the Cohere 402 cascade a feature of this project due to its linguistic nature, or is it a structural property of civic AI tools built for non-English linguistic contexts?

Where This Goes Next

The tool exists, the repository, despite being incomplete, is public, the pipeline is documented, the architecture is reproducible by a developer with institutional infrastructure capable of hosting 15GB of OCR data, this thesis, as an account of the frustration one can encounter, is nearly completed. A future iteration with adequate funding, native Arabic legal literacy at the evaluation stage, and a full decade of the Gazette issue in conjunction with the weekly current issue releases would move this from a proof of concept toward something that could actually serve the citizens it was designed for.

The oscillating method could be applied to other civic technology interventions, such as researchers rebuilding government archives, developers building judicial transparency tools, or making state regulatory records searchable in languages the major models were not trained to handle well. Comparative work across multiple cases would begin the process of establishing which findings were specific to Lebanon and which of those findings are simply inherent structural properties of the current AI landscape more broadly. In addition, the oscillating method itself would merit being developed into a plugin or broader framework designed for civic technology development, forcing the human developer to understand and question what the system is doing, how it is doing it, and why it is doing it.

The specific finding regarding the Cohere 402 event and its linguistic cascade suggests a narrower, but urgent research agenda: what happens to the quality and accessibility of AI-assisted civic tools in non-English contexts when corporate billing thresholds are crossed, or payment is not met? As more civil society organizations build on commercial API’s without institutional funding, this does not constitute an abstract question; it has direct implications for digital rights, information access, and the governance of AI infrastructure in the Global South.

This project was not proof that building civic infrastructure with AI is possible, nor was it proof that it was impossible; instead, it was a demonstration of the costs that are incumbent to these projects. In terms of labor, capital, compromise, and incompleteness, and more importantly, that those costs be made visible. The hype discourse promises frictionless democratization, but the friction encountered throughout this project is the finding.

On a Personal Note

In collecting the Data for this thesis, I stumbled on a voice recording I had made on the 13th of May 2026, in which I explain to myself that the conclusion to this project, aside from the thesis itself, is that structurally, it does not matter how good or intelligent the LLM or coding tools become, or how much agency they provide. If the structural foundations are not there or are eroded, the AI is not going to solve the problem. In this instance, disregarding the paywall, the core issue is the format. And adjacent to the format are those who formatted it.

Artificial Intelligence, for all the good it may bring, and all the bad it has done, will not solve corrupted power structures, self-interest, and complacency. We remain the ones who decide, act, prompt, or build.

In so doing, having submitted on time, I will return to Lebanon and present my findings. This tool will see the light of day, perhaps not in its current form, but in the ideology that has carried it. That is to say, dignity.
